# Enjoying European Football: A Beginner’s Guide for Americans

by [Wayland Bruns](https://www.dejaoffice.com/blog/author/wbclcto/ "View all posts by Wayland Bruns")

Nothing in America can match the ardor and fervor found in European football. Football fans from Lisbon to Vladivostok, from Stockholm to Buenos Ares, view football as essential to their sporting life. They intently watch matches and cheer their favorite players. It is vital in Europe. A lot of people in the United States are switching to it. Resources like <https://footballmatchonline.com/en/> make it easy to tune in, so a small taste can bring you to becoming the newest football fan.

Unlike American football, the European game is much simpler, not as encrusted by high-tech equipment, and lets you enjoy the raw power of the players. European football leagues like the English Premier League and La Liga are popular among Americans.

European football has many tournaments. The domestic league is where teams in one country play against each other. The Champions League is for the top teams from different countries. The Europa League is for other teams to compete.

European football clubs often come from different parts of a country. For example, the English Premier League has teams from cities in England and also Wales. These clubs hire players from around the world, so the sport mixes many cultures.

You feel like you are watching a drama when you see European football. Every game has a story because players from all over the world play for their clubs. You will like a team or player if you learn the basics and want to see them play again.

## The most loved European Football Leagues

In the world, people love football the most. The United States calls it soccer. Europe has very competitive leagues. Each league is special with its teams and players. Here are five top European football leagues.

### The English Premier League

People all over the world watch the English Premier League the most. Famous clubs like Manchester United, Liverpool, and Arsenal play here. This league’s games are fast and strong, and the fans really care.

### The Spanish La Liga

La Liga is famous for smooth, skillful and intense games. Big Spanish clubs like Real Madrid and FC Barcelona play here. Atletico Madrid, Valencia, and Sevilla are other important teams. The league has great players like Lionel Messi, Cristiano Ronaldo, and Andres Iniesta.

### The German Bundesliga

The Bundesliga is known for its quick games and pressing style. Bayern Munich has won a lot in the last ten years, but other teams like Borussia Dortmund, RB Leipzig, and Bayer Leverkusen are trying to win now, too. The Italian Serie A

Serie A is home to the world’s best defenders and goalkeepers. It is known for its defensive play style. Juventus dominate Serie A, but AC Milan, Inter Milan, Lazio, and Roma are close behind.

### The French Ligue 1

Ligue 1’s big team is Paris Saint-Germain. They have been on top for ten years. Monaco, Marseille, and Lyon get better, making the league more competitive.

### Key Players and Teams

Famous footballers like Lionel Messi, Cristiano Ronaldo, Neymar Jr., Kylian Mbappe, and Kevin de Bruyne are big stars. They play for the biggest clubs in Europe. Clubs like Real Madrid, Barcelona, Bayern Munich, and Manchester City win lots of trophies.

## How to Choose a Team to Support

Choosing a team to support can be hard. It depends on where you live, the team’s history, their players, and how they play. When you support a team, it brings out your passion and helps you make friends.

## Watching the Games

Watching European football can be hard for American fans because of time zones. Fans can watch the games on NBC Sports, ESPN+, or FuboTV. Bars and sports pubs are good places for live football.

## Summing Up European Football: Why Americans Should Watch and Enjoy

European football leagues, key players and teams, and how to watch games shows it is exciting. Americans should watch and enjoy European football, even if they did not grow up with the sport. Exploring the Different Leagues and Teams

Each top European football league has unique features. The English Premier League is known for intense matches. Spain’s La Liga shows cases of technical skill. Italy’s Serie A focuses on defense. These leagues have big clubs in the EPL, like Manchester United and Liverpool. Paris Saint-Germain is now very strong in Ligue 1.

## Why Americans Should Tune In

Soccer in Europe might be less popular in the US than American football or basketball. But there are good reasons to watch it. It is a fun sport for everyone. You do not need to know a lot about it to enjoy it. Cheering for a team helps you make friends. The excitement of European football is different from American sports. The game and its culture are special.

title: Beginner's guide to European soccer | Sports | laloyolan.com
content:
Cartoon: Parker Alexander | Loyolan

For many American sports fans, the thought of watching European soccer can be daunting. This is in part due to the many leagues, tournaments and players that make soccer in Europe extremely different from many sports in the United States.

To assist, I have created a beginner's guide to watching European soccer. I should note that I have chosen to focus on European soccer, as it is, in my opinion, the best starting point for fans getting into soccer.

The governing bodies

In Europe, specifically, the governing body for the sport is the Union of European Football Associations (UEFA), which is governed by FIFA. UEFA governs 55 national associations and is the overseeing federation for all top-flight club and national team competitions.

The leagues [...] Instead of having a playoff system like most U.S. sports leagues, the winner is determined through the point system where a win gives you three points, a draw gives you one point and a loss gives you no points. By the end of the season, the point leader at the top of the table wins the league.

Outside of their primary league, Manchester City also competes in multiple cups and tournaments that take place both within England and the rest of Europe. In England specifically, teams have opportunities to play in the FA Cup and Carabao Cup, two tournaments that include teams from multiple levels of the English system, but tournaments like this exist for many other countries as well. [...] This reputation of excellence isn't without good reason, as many of the best players in national team competitions play for a team in one of these leagues. Legends like Diego Maradona, Lionel Messi, Ronaldinho Gaucho and David Beckham have all competed in these leagues.

In my opinion, the best league to watch as a new fan is the Premier League, since it houses many well-known clubs and is arguably the most accessible league in the world. No matter which league you are first drawn to, there is no bad place to start.

While these leagues have their own unique charms, what truly makes European soccer so great are the clubs themselves.

The clubs

![champs league cartoon](data:image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAAAAQAAAADCAQAAAAe/WZNAAAAEElEQVR42mM8U88ABowYDABAxQPltt5zqAAAAABJRU5ErkJggg==)

Soccer has so many different leagues, cups and tournaments that it can become very confusing for new viewers.

  [Cartoon: Parker Alexander | Loyolan](https://www.laloyolan.com/users/profile/Parker%20Alexander)

For many American sports fans, the thought of watching European soccer can be daunting. This is in part due to the many leagues, tournaments and players that make soccer in Europe extremely different from many sports in the United States.

To assist, I have created a beginner's guide to watching European soccer. I should note that I have chosen to focus on European soccer, as it is, in my opinion, the best starting point for fans getting into soccer.

**The governing bodies**

In Europe, specifically, the governing body for the sport is the Union of European Football Associations ([UEFA](https://www.uefa.com/insideuefa/about-uefa/)), which is governed by [FIFA](https://www.fifa.com/about-fifa/associations). UEFA governs 55 national associations and is the overseeing federation for all top-flight club and national team competitions.

**The leagues**

There are many leagues in Europe that are considered to be amongst the very best in the world. However, the leagues that deserve the most attention are the big five: the English Premier League, the French Ligue 1, the Italian Serie A, the German Bundesliga and the Spanish La Liga.

Each league plays host to the strongest club teams in the world. Players from all over the globe work for years to make it to these leagues as, for many fans, players and soccer purists, these leagues are seen as the be-all and end-all.

This reputation of excellence isn't without good reason, as many of the best players in national team competitions play for a team in one of these leagues. Legends like Diego Maradona, Lionel Messi, Ronaldinho Gaucho and David Beckham have all competed in these leagues.

In my opinion, the best league to watch as a new fan is the Premier League, since it houses many well-known clubs and is arguably the most accessible league in the world. No matter which league you are first drawn to, there is no bad place to start.

While these leagues have their own unique charms, what truly makes European soccer so great are the clubs themselves.

**The clubs**

If you follow sports in any capacity, you will have definitely heard the names FC Barcelona, Real Madrid and Liverpool. There are so many amazing clubs scattered around these five leagues from powerful giants like Bayern Munich and Paris Saint-Germain to smaller clubs like Brentford and Spezia Calcio.

No matter who you are or what you enjoy, there is a club for everyone to enjoy. However, choosing a European club to latch on to can be overwhelming for a new fan, so don’t be afraid to do your research and find the club that speaks to you.

To me, diving into the history and tradition of a club is one of the most fun things for soccer fans to do and is partly why I decided to create this guide in the first place. I should note, though, not all clubs are created equal. For many clubs, experiencing any form of long-term success at the highest levels is nothing short of a dream, but for a select few of elite, historical and oftentimes-wealthy clubs, this success is expected and achieved.

While this should not deter you from becoming a die-hard Southampton fan, it is important to keep in mind when choosing your future soccer fandom.

**The competitions**

This is where explaining European soccer can become a little confusing for many fans of American sports leagues. Here in the U.S., sports leagues generally function as the sole governing body and have one primary championship that is up for grabs every year and decided via a playoff.

European soccer works a little differently, to say the least. To explain this, I am going to talk from the lens of the Premier League and one of its members, Manchester City. Manchester City, as an English team, competes in the Premier League.

Instead of having a playoff system like most U.S. sports leagues, the winner is determined through the point system where a win gives you three points, a draw gives you one point and a loss gives you no points. By the end of the season, the point leader at the top of [the table](https://www.espn.com/soccer/table/_/league/eng.1) wins the league.

Outside of their primary league, Manchester City also competes in multiple cups and tournaments that take place both within England and the rest of Europe. In England specifically, teams have opportunities to play in the [FA Cup](https://www.thefa.com/competitions/thefacup) and [Carabao Cup](https://www.efl.com/carabao-cup/), two tournaments that include teams from multiple levels of the English system, but tournaments like this exist for many other countries as well.

If you perform extremely well in your primary league, you get the opportunity to compete in European competitions. The biggest of these competitions is the [UEFA Champions League](https://www.uefa.com/uefachampionsleague/), which sets out to determine who the best team in Europe is every year. For smaller clubs, the goal is to be in the running for their league title and maybe even one of their countries’ cups, but for the biggest clubs, they start their year with sights pointed directly at the Champions League trophy.

Obviously, this is a lot to take in for fans who are not used to this system yet, but with some time, much of this begins to make more sense.

**General tips**

Even for me, soccer can be difficult to keep up with or understand, so I am always trying to find ways to keep up with this beautiful sport.

Getting a streaming service like ESPN+ or Peacock opens the gates to a lot of high-quality soccer, but there are lots of other ways to enjoy the game. From reading articles to watching YouTube videos talking about the latest title races or transfer rumors, there is never too much soccer.

Beyond that, I would suggest continuing to branch out and exploring what the world of soccer has to offer. Whether that is watching the LMU soccer teams next season, enjoying some MLS or playing FIFA, expanding your horizons with the world's most popular sport is always a good thing. While I do believe these five leagues are the best place to start, I don’t think they should be the only soccer you pay attention to, with dozens of amazing leagues around the globe to enjoy.

This is the opinion of **Jacob Rincon**, a freshman screenwriting major from El Paso, Texas. Email comments to [editor@theloyolan.com](mailto:editor@theloyolan.com). Tweet comments to @LALoyolan and like the Loyolan on Facebook.

## What is football?

Football, which is also often referred to as soccer outside the UK and association football throughout Europe, is a game of two teams and one ball – the football. Each team has 11 players and, using all parts of their bodies apart from their arms and hands, must try to get the ball into the other team’s goal.

The goal is, typically, a large rectangular frame with a net attached to the back. However, amateur games often just use markers such as traffic cones or, if you’re a teenager, sweaters or bags. When it’s not professional football, the rules are often relaxed. [...] UK & Ireland

Europe

International

There are many other leagues out there, but the above are the ones you’ll often hear talked about on television, in football publications and down the pub.

## In summary

Football in various forms spans centuries. It’s the most popular game and sport in the world, watched by billions and played by millions every year. There are hundreds of thousands of football clubs around the world – including local teams you can join. If you’re someone with a flare for competition, it could be the game for you.

# What is football? The beginner’s guide to the beautiful game

![What is football? The beginner's guide to the beautiful game](https://thepfsa.co.uk/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/1444223035863.jpeg)

The Fédération Internationale de Football Association (FIFA), the body that governs the game, estimates that more than 1.3 billion people are actively interested in the football around the world, which makes football the most popular game in the world by a mile.

Decades ago, something about football took the world by storm and turned what a simple game into a lifestyle – a competitive sport that would see the most talented players being paid millions per game. But what makes it so special?

## What is football?

Football, which is also often referred to as soccer outside the UK and association football throughout Europe, is a game of two teams and one ball – the football. Each team has 11 players and, using all parts of their bodies apart from their arms and hands, must try to get the ball into the other team’s goal.

The goal is, typically, a large rectangular frame with a net attached to the back. However, amateur games often just use markers such as traffic cones or, if you’re a teenager, sweaters or bags. When it’s not professional football, the [rules](https://thepfsa.co.uk/how-to-play-football/) are often relaxed.

It’s the goalkeeper’s job to stop the football going into the net. Each team has a goalkeeper who, as an exception, can use their hands to obstruct the ball. Although, they’re only allowed to do so in the penalty area – a small area in front of and around the goal.

In football, the team that scores the most goals by the end of the game wins. The standard length of a game is 90 minutes with a short break, known as ‘half time’, at the 45-minute mark. However, youth football games are often shorter – typically, they only last 45-60 minutes.

## Where can you play football?

Professional football games always take place on a full-size pitch, which must measure 90-120 metres (m) in length, with a width of 45-90m. However, football can be played anywhere.

Since the game was first played, it has been played in the streets, gardens, community areas, school halls and even in war zones.

One of the most famous examples of football being played under unlikely circumstances in the First World War and the Christmas truce of 1914, where German and British armed forces left their trenches and entered No Man’s Land, where they’re reported to have played football and sung carols for just one day, before resuming fighting.

## The history of the game

Football has a detailed history that spans centuries. Some of the most important moments for the game since it was first played include:

**Signs of football in China in the third century BC**

History books states that the Han Dynasty played Tsu’ Chu, a game with remarkable similarities to football. It involved kicking a feather-stuffed leather ball through a small opening made from cane and netting.

**‘Harpastum’, the Greek’s take on football**

Greek texts describe a game in which two teams had to try to get the ball over the other team’s boundary line. The game was played on a rectangular field with a marked centre line, which sounds very similar to modern football pitches.

**Football chaos in Britain in 1314 and beyond**

The Lord Mayor of London banned football, or a similar game as it was known then, in 1314. You could even be sent to prison if you were caught playing football in the city.

**Football as a distraction**

For the 100 years France and Britain were at war, football was deemed illegal as it made soldiers unfocused.

**The modern embrace of football**

After centuries of negativity surrounding the game, football thrived in the 19th century and beyond. Villages would come together to play against each other, which is still very much the case today.

**Independence in football in 1863**

Association football and rugby football used to be under the same umbrella, but they were separated in 1863, which is when the Football Association was born.

**Standardising the ball**

In 1871, the specifications of the ball used in football were set by the Football Association and have, near enough, stayed the same since.

**FIFA takes over**

The Association was founded in Paris in 1904 and now dictates the sport, with more than 73 members. It’s since FIFA was started that leagues have been standardised and football has become a global phenomenon. It has been growing ever since; the Association now has 208 international members.

Of course, there are many other significant moments in football, but it’s the above that, for the most part, gets us to where we are today.

There are more than 600 football clubs in England alone, which means there could be hundreds of thousands of clubs across the world.

## Essential football leagues

There are a variety of essential football leagues you should be aware of, including:

**UK & Ireland**

**Europe**

**International**

There are many other leagues out there, but the above are the ones you’ll often hear talked about on television, in football publications and down the pub.

## In summary

Football in various forms spans centuries. It’s the most popular game and sport in the world, watched by billions and played by millions every year. There are hundreds of thousands of football clubs around the world – including local teams you can join. If you’re someone with a flare for competition, it could be the game for you.

# How is European soccer structured with leagues and cup competitions?

The structure of European football is complex. Although governing body UEFA oversees continental competitions, individual nations are able to operate their domestic leagues and cups in a manner best suited to the country. This has created a range of differing leagues, as well as qualification processes for the UEFA Champions League, Europa League and Conference League.

There are 55 national association members in UEFA. Some of those are not sovereign nations, such as Gibraltar and the Faroe Islands, while sovereign states like Monaco and the Vatican City are not represented. Some, such as Kazakhstan, are not geographically recognised as being in Europe. For some people, that's like having Australia compete in Eurovision…

Imagine if all 50 US states, plus territories like Guam and Puerto Rico, had their own governing bodies deciding how their soccer leagues – consisting of numerous levels – should operate. That is European football.

![](/assets/placeholder/resp_live_placeholder_image_16_9.svg)

Bayern Munich became the only German club to truly master Europe’s complex system when it won the Bundesliga, DFB Cup and UEFA Champions League, as well as the Supercup, under Jupp Heynckes in 2012/13.  - 2013 Getty Images

**So how are domestic leagues structured?**

With the exception of the small Alpine nation of Liechtenstein, all UEFA members run their own domestic league system. This is sometimes referred to as the 'pyramid', with a nationwide first division at the top. Below that, as the pyramid widens, is where things differ. Depending on population size and the number of clubs, divisions will either remain nationwide or eventually split to become regional. That creates the pyramid shape in a diagram.

Focusing on the top tier, the most common format consists of each team playing the other twice – once at home and once away – from fall to spring. Others in colder climates will perhaps run within a single calendar year or have an extended winter break to avoid playing in freezing conditions. In any case, it's three points for a win, one for a draw, and the team with the most points after all the games is the champion.

When it comes to teams tied for points, leagues define criteria to determine who is ahead. The first of those is often who has the higher goal difference (goals scored minus goals conceded) over the course of the season, followed by goals scored and so on. Some leagues, however, use the head-to-head record between rival teams as the first criteria after points, including away goals, before continuing with other season-spanning statistics. This has been known to go as far as the team with the fewest yellow cards. If needed, teams can also be separated by an additional one-off match, the drawing of lots, or a coin toss.

What may be considered the 'regular season' in the USA is in fact just 'the season' in most European leagues. For them, any reference to the 'play-offs' is usually to do with promotion and relegation (more on that later).

In the Bundesliga, 18 teams play a total of 34 matches each – two against each team, once at home and once away – in a random order set out by the fixture list. The English Premier League, Spain's La Liga, Italy's Serie A and France's Ligue 1 – the other leagues considered Europe's "big five" – all consist of 20 teams, resulting in 38 rounds of fixtures.

There is no one-size-fits-all approach, though. Scotland, for example, has three parts to its 'regular season'. Since the Scottish Premiership consists of only 12 teams, the first 22 rounds of fixtures see them play the customary home and away match against each other. They then play each other a third time, either home or away depending on how the fixtures are drawn.

Once 33 games have been played, the league 'splits' into two halves of six teams. Each club will then play five more matches against the other teams in their half. The champion is again the team that finishes with the most points, but teams cannot leave their half of the table. This can result in an odd situation where seventh place may have more points than sixth come the end of 38 games.

**Watch:** *Promotion highs, relegation lows in 2016/17*

Watch: Promotion highs, relegation lows

Things are even more complex in Belgium. After 30 rounds of fixtures in the 16-team league, the top six then enter the championship play-offs. Their regular season points are halved as they begin a new mini league, playing home and away against each other to determine the champion and also European qualifiers.

Teams seventh to 12th compete in the Europe play-offs, with the winner there competing against fourth from the championship play-off to get into continental competition. The bottom four contest the relegation play-off. The bottom two go down, with third-bottom then playing another play-off against the winner of the promotion play-off from the second division.

The point is, each league can do things how they want.

**What is promotion and relegation?**

The great difference with soccer in the USA and Major League Soccer, however, is the system of promotion and relegation.

In Europe, teams move between levels of the pyramid at the end of each season. That means a set number of clubs at the bottom end of a division (except in the bottom-most league) will drop into the one below. Sides finishing at the top end of all leagues – bar the top tier – will move up a level.

It will be no surprise, though, that things aren't quite that simple and are rarely uniform across leagues and countries. For example, [the Bundesliga has two automatic promotion and relegation places](https://www.bundesliga.com/en/bundesliga/news/how-does-bundesliga-promotion-and-relegation-work-play-off-4061). The top two sides at the end of the Bundesliga 2 campaign therefore replace the bottom two in the Bundesliga.

![](/assets/placeholder/resp_live_placeholder_image_16_9.svg)

Joshua Kimmich was one of just four players who spent every single second of his team’s 34 Bundesliga games in 2018/19 on the pitch.  - imago images / Sven Simon

There is one more spot available that isn't automatic. Since 2008, the team finishing third from bottom in the Bundesliga faces the side third in Bundesliga 2 over a two-legged play-off. The winner plays in the Bundesliga the following year. A similar practice is used in France's Ligue 1, although the team third from bottom there will face the winner of a series of play-offs between the sides finishing third to fifth in Ligue 2.

Across the remainder of Europe's "big five", they use another slightly different method. In the Premier League, La Liga and Serie A, the bottom three teams are automatically relegated, but there are just two automatic promotion positions from the league below – the Championship, Segunda División, and Serie B respectively.

In England, the third team in the second-tier Championship plays sixth while fourth faces fifth in two-legged semi-finals, before a one-off final at Wembley to determine the third promoted team. In Spain, both semi-finals and the final consist of two legs.

![](/assets/placeholder/resp_live_placeholder_image_16_9.svg)

Jürgen Klopp also lifted the national cup, the DFB Cup, during his time with Borussia Dortmund, going with two Bundesliga titles in the league.  - 2012 AFP

In Italy, teams third through eighth in Serie B enter the play-offs. Fifth plays eighth and sixth hosts seventh over one leg. The winners then face a two-legged clash with either third or fourth, with the victors of those semi-finals contesting a two-legged final.

Similar patterns are found throughout the continent, and play-offs are usually seen as end-of-season highlights before the summer break.

**Are the cups linked to the leagues?**

While the MLS Cup is what decides the league's champion at the end of the regular season and play-offs, European cup competitions are nothing to do with the domestic leagues.

The limit of a league's involvement in a cup is often at what stage certain teams enter the draw, or who they can be drawn against.

![](/assets/placeholder/resp_live_placeholder_image_16_9.svg)

Bayern Munich consistent continental performances ensure they are usually at the top end of the UEFA coefficient ranking for clubs, as is the Bundesliga for league rankings.  - imago sportfotodienst

National cups are predominantly run by the national association, who nowadays tend to no longer run the top domestic leagues. In Germany, for example, the DFL (Deutsche Fußball Liga) controls the Bundesliga and Bundesliga 2, while the DFB (Deutscher Fußball-Bund, or German FA) organises the nationwide third division and the cup – the [DFB Cup](-bayern-munich-borussia-dortmund-rb-leipzig-23821) (DFB-Pokal). Below the third tier, regional associations organise leagues under the umbrella of the DFB.

The national cup holds great prestige for teams and has often been in existence longer than the league system. Cups are usually knockout tournaments over one or two legs, culminating in an end-of-season final. The winners are often also rewarded with European qualification if not already achieved through their league position (more on that later).

A team that wins both the top division and cup in their country is said to have won the domestic double. In Germany this has happened 17 times, with [Bayern Munich](https://www.bundesliga.com/en/bundesliga/clubs/fc-bayern-muenchen/news) completing a league and cup double on 13 occasions. Three trophies is a treble, four a quadruple and so on.

There are sometimes also secondary cups. A league cup, organised by the league association, was once common but has started to fall out of fashion. From 2020/21, England will be the only nation among the top five to still hold a league cup.

![](/assets/placeholder/resp_live_placeholder_image_16_9.svg)

Julian Nagelsmann and Hoffenheim benefited from an extra automatic qualification spot in 2018/19, having fallen to Liverpool in the play-offs the previous season after finishing fourth.  - AMELIE QUERFURTH/AFP via Getty Images

There are also cups available solely to teams outside of the top division(s), or regional cups, often for non-professional clubs. Eligibility for these competitions, though, is as linked as leagues get to most cups.

And a special mention again to Liechtenstein. Although its teams compete in the Swiss league system, it does have its own domestic cup competition, allowing clubs from one of the world's two doubly landlocked countries a chance to compete in the Europa League under their national flag.

That is different from AS Monaco, though. Monaco is an independent nation but – due to its size – it has no professional domestic league or cup. This means that the club plays in the French system and represents France in Europe.

**How does European qualification work?**

'Qualification for Europe' may sound an odd phrase when you already play in Europe. It refers to being able to compete in one of the continental club competitions organised by UEFA. This does not include the European Championship, known as the Euros, which is for national teams.

![](/assets/placeholder/resp_live_placeholder_image_16_9.svg)

Eintracht Frankfurt were given a place in Europa League qualifiers despite finishing seventh because Bayern also won the DFB Cup in 2018/19.  - AFP/Getty Images

There are currently three Europe-wide club competitions: the Champions League, the Europa League and the Conference League.

This is not to be confused with a domestic pyramid structure. There is no promotion or relegation between these leagues. Participation is based on the team's performances the previous season.

Qualification for these competitions is primarily decided by domestic league positions, although domestic cup competitions also usually offer a route into Europe. All this, however, depends on a country's UEFA coefficient.

This is a ranking based on the performances of clubs from each league in European competition over a set period. The higher ranked a league is, the more European qualification berths they're allotted.

Some of these berths earn teams the right to go straight into the competition itself, beginning in the group stage. Others still have to go through qualifying – consisting of up to five rounds – to reach that stage. So qualification for Europe sometimes means qualifying for the chance to qualify for Europe.

![](/assets/placeholder/resp_live_placeholder_image_16_9.svg)

The Bundesliga has been a highly successful league in the UEFA Champions League - European football premier club competition.  - imago

For rules for the Champions League change in 2024 as a new format is introduced and the competition expanded.

The base ruling is that the holders and previous Europa League winners are joined by the champions of the top 10 ranked nations, the six runners-up from the top six, and the third-placed teams from the top five and also the fourth-placed teams from the top four. There will also be two addiitonal berths granted to the associations with the highest coefficients from the previous season, i.e. the Bundesliga could get five teams in the Champions League if its clubs performed well the previous year.

Should the holders of the two European competitions already qualify via their league position, that opens up the opportunity for an extra club from another league to gain automatic entry.

The remaining aeven places are made up of teams that have gone through the qualification process.

The 30 champions from associations ranked 25-55 (except league-less Liechtenstein) contest the first round. The 15 winners are joined by the nine champions from associations 15-24 (currently except Russia due to suspension) in the 'champions path' and then the six runners-up from associations 10-15 in the 'league path'.

The twelve winners from the champions path go into the third round, while the three league path winners are joined by the three runners-up from associations 7-9, the third-place team from association 6 and the fourth-placed team from association 5.

The six champions path winners then contest the play-off in addition to the four champions from associations 11-14, with the winners going into the group stage. As do the four winners from the league path.

**[How does European qualification work in Germany](https://www.bundesliga.com/en/bundesliga/news/how-many-bundesliga-teams-can-qualify-for-europe-19689)?**

With the Bundesliga currently ranked among Europe's top four leagues, German clubs finishing in the top four can avoid an arduous qualification process by entering straight into the group stage.

Winning a domestic cup does not earn you a place in the Champions League, but it is one way of reaching the Europa League – formerly known as the UEFA Cup.

![](/assets/placeholder/resp_live_placeholder_image_16_9.svg)

RB Leipzig reached the knockout stages of the UEFA Champions League for the first time in their history in 2019/20.  - Martin Rose/Bongarts/Getty Images

A similarly long qualification process is also needed there, consisting of a mix of cup winners from lower-ranked nations, teams finishing between second and sixth in their domestic league (depending on association ranking) and teams knocked out of Champions League qualification.

In Bundesliga terms, though, fifth place goes directly into the group stage alongside the DFB Cup winners, while sixth place goes into Conference League qualifying round. This is assuming that the Bundesliga does not get its additional fifth Champions League berth.

Should the DFB Cup winners finish fifth or better in the Bundesliga, the team in sixth will automatically enter the Europa League and seventh place into the Conference League.

It's a complicated process for those teams playing in lower-ranked leagues, but relatively straightforward for those in the Bundesliga due to the league's high standing on the continent.

**What is a super cup?**

So, there are leagues, cups, Champions Leagues, Europa Leagues, Conference Leagues – but what about a super cup, sometimes also written as 'Supercup'?

There are both domestic and continental super cups.

For the latter, it consists of a one-off match on neutral ground between the previous winners of the Champions League and Europa League, being played early the following season. Note that the winner of the Champions League represents Europe at the FIFA Club World Cup regardless of the outcome of the UEFA Super Cup.

Domestically, not every country will hold a form of super cup, but it is generally contested between the winners of the league and the cup. In Germany, that sees the winners of the DFB Cup traditionally host the Bundesliga champions in the Supercup the week before the main season begins. In the event that one team wins both, the Bundesliga runners-up are next in line to compete.

**Watch:** *Highlights of the season-opening 2019 Supercup between Dortmund and Bayern*

Watch: 2019 DFL Supercup highlights

The same happens in England, with the Community Shield played at Wembley between the Premier League champions and FA Cup winners in August.

In Italy, the Supercoppa Italiana is now generally held abroad and features the cup runner-up in the event of a domestic double. It is played either the week before the new season or in December/January, depending on its location.

Spain previously held their Supercopa over two legs. After one year with a one-off match at a neutral venue, they have now introduced a new format featuring four teams contesting one-legged semi-finals and a final featuring the Copa del Rey finalists and the next two highest-ranked league teams.

All in all, as is so often the case in Europe, things differ in every country. The principle remains the same, with domestic silverware and European qualification on the line. Knowing how teams in a specific country achieve that, however, requires a little bit of research.

# Football Terms

Football, or soccer as it's known in some countries, is a sport rich with unique terminology that reflects its global reach and tactical complexity. Whether you're a seasoned fan or new to the game, understanding these key terms can enhance your appreciation of the strategies, rules, and culture that define the beautiful game.

![goalkeeper dive](images/goal-keeper-dive.png)football dive

* **Attacker:** A player whose job is to play the ball forward towards the opponent's goal area to create a scoring opportunity.
* **Back Heel:** A ball kicked using the back (heel) of the foot.
* **Back Pass:** A pass that a player makes back toward their own goal, usually made back to the goalkeeper. This is often a defensive move to restart a new phase of play.
* **Ball Carrier:** The player in possession of the ball.
* **Bending the Ball:** Striking the ball off-center so that it travels in a curved path, ideally for shots at goal.
* **Bicycle Kick:** A spectacular move in which a player jumps in the air in a backflip motion, kicking the ball backward over their head. The name comes from action which mimics their legs moving as if pedaling a bicycle.
* **Center Spot:** The spot marked at the center of the field from which the kickoff is made.
* **Confederation:** Organization responsible for football in their region (see [acronyms](federations.htm))
* **Corner Flag:** The flag marking each of the four corners of the field.
* **Corner Kick:** A free kick taken from the corner of the field by an attacker. The corner kick is awarded when the ball has passed over the goal line after last touching a defensive player. The shot is taken from the corner nearest to where the ball went out.
* **Cross:** A pass played across the face of a goal.

* **Defender:** A player whose job is to stop the opposition attacking players from goal scoring.
* **Direct Free Kick:** A free kick in which a goal may be scored by the player taking the free kick.
* **Dribble:** Keeping control of the ball while running.
* **Dummy Run:** A run by a player without the ball, to lure defenders away from the ball carrier.
* **Far Post:** The goalpost farthest from the ball.
* **FIFA:** The acronym used for the Federation Internationale de Football Association, the world governing body for the game of association football, which is based in Switzerland.
* **Football:** The term used for soccer in Europe and other countries outside of North America and Australia.
* **Foul:** Any illegal play.
* **Free Kick:** A kick awarded to an opposition player when an player has committed a foul. Free kicks can be either direct or indirect.
* **[Futsal](../list/futsal.htm):** version of Fottball played indoors.
* **Give and Go:** (also known as a 1-2) When a player passes the ball to a teammate, who immediately one-touch passes the ball back to the first player.
* **Goal Area:** The rectangular area in front of the goal. It is also known as the 6-yard box because of its dimensions.
* **Goal Kick:** A goal kick is awarded to the defending team when the ball is played over the goal line by the attacking team. It can taken by any player though it is normally taken by the goalkeeper.
* **Goal Line:** The two boundary lines located at each end of the field.
* **Goal Mouth:** The area in front of the goal.
* **Goalkeeper:** The specialized player who is the last line of defense, who is allowed to control the ball with his hands when in the goal area.
* **Header:** Using of the head to pass or control the ball.
* **Indirect Free Kick:** A free kick awarded to a player from which a goal may not be scored directly.
* **Inswinger:** A kick that curves in toward the goal.
* **Kickoff:** The kickoff is taken from the center spot at the start of play at the beginning of each half and after a goal has been scored.
* **Man to Man Marking:** A defensive system where defenders are designated one attacking player to track continuously.
* **Midfielder:** The playing position for players that are responsible for linking play between attackers and defenders.
* **Nearpost:** The goalpost nearest the ball.
* **Obstruction:** Causing obstruction, which is blocking an opponent with the body, is penalized by awarding an indirect free kick to the opposition.
* **Offside Trap:** A technique used by defenders to put attacking players in an offside position, by moving quickly away from their own goal to leave attackers offside.
* **Offside:** A player is in an offside position if he is nearer to his opponent's goal line than both the ball and the second-to-last opponent. This does not apply if the players is is on their half of the field. An indirect free kick is awarded to the opposing team at the place where the offside occurred.
* **One-Touch Pass:** A pass in which the ball is played on with a player's first touch.
* **Out Swinger:** A kick that swerves away from the goal.
* **Penalty Area:** The rectangular area in front of the goal in which the goalkeeper may handle the ball. It is also known as the 18-yard box because of its dimensions.
* **Penalty Spot:** The marked spot 12 yards from the goal line from which a penalty kick is taken.
* **Penalty:** A penalty kick is awarded when a foul has been committed inside the penalty area in front of the goal. A penalty is taken by one player opposed only by the goal keeper.
* **Pitch:** The soccer field of play.
* **Red Card:** A red card is issued to a player when that player has committed a serious infraction or has been issued with two yellow cards within the same game. The red card held up by the referee to signal that a player is being sent off. The player sent off cannot be replaced.
* **Referee:** The official who is in charge of the game.
* **Shot:** A kick, header, or any intended deflection of the ball toward a goal by a player attempting to score a goal.
* **Sliding Tackle:** A tackle in which the defender slides along the surface of the field of play before making one-footed contact with the ball.
* **Striker:** An attacking player whose job is to finish attacking plays by scoring a goal.
* **Sweeper:** A defensive player whose job is to roam behind the other defenders.  
   A sweeper has no specific marking duties and is the last line of defense before the goalkeeper.
* **Tackle:** To take the ball away from the opponent using the feet.
* **Through Pass:** A pass played past defenders into free space to allow a teammate to run onto the ball.
* **Throw-In:** The ball is thrown in after the ball has crossed the touch line. A player taking a throw in must have both feet on or behind the touch line, must maintain contact with the ground, and must use a two-handed throw made from behind the head. A goal cannot be score directly from a throw-in.
* **Toe Poke:** Use of the toe to strike the ball.
* **Touch Line:** The line that defines the outer edge of the longer sides of the field of play.
* **Trapping the Ball:** Controlling the ball with the sole of the foot.
* **Volley:** Striking the ball in mid-air with either foot.
* **Wingers:** Attackers who play on the wings/flanks of the field.
* **Yellow Card:** A yellow card is held up by a referee to signal a caution for a minor infringement.
* **Zone Defense:** A defensive system where defenders mark a designated area of the field of play instead of tracking players across the field.

title: European League of Football - Wikipedia
content:
The European League of Football (ELF) is a professional American football league based in Europe, continuing some team names of the former NFL Europe. The ELF played its first season in 2021, and has 16 teams as of the 2025 season. Teams are located in Austria, the Czech Republic, Denmark, France, Hungary, Germany, Poland, Spain, and Switzerland with plans to expand.

## History

[edit]

The European League of Football was announced 4 November 2020 by Patrick Esume, former American Football coach and TV commentator, and sports manager Zeljko Karajica. Since 2018, Esume had criticized the American Football Association of Germany (AFVD) for not using the fan potential in Germany, and tried to set up a professionalized structure for American football in Germany and the rest of Europe. | Chess |  Men   + European Chess Club Cup  Women   + European Chess Club Cup |
| Rugby league |  Men   + European Super League |
| Lacrosse |  Men / Women   + Ken Galluccio Cup |
| Judo |  Men / Women   + Champions League "Champions League (judo)")   + Europa League "Europa League (Judo)") | [...] |  IFAF  World Championship  Women's World Championship  World Championship Flag football  Junior World Championship  World University Championship  Olympics (1904, 1932, 2028 flag)  World Games (2005, 2017, 2022 flag, 2025 flag) |

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Professional American football league

This article is about the American football league. For the association football leagues, see [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA").

Not to be confused with defunct competitions such as the [European Football League](/wiki/European_Football_League "European Football League"), [BIG6 European Football League](/wiki/BIG6_European_Football_League "BIG6 European Football League") and [NFL Europe](/wiki/NFL_Europe "NFL Europe").

The **European League of Football** (**ELF**) is a [professional](/wiki/Professional_gridiron_football "Professional gridiron football")[[2]](#cite_note-2) [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") league based in Europe, continuing some team names of the former [NFL Europe](/wiki/NFL_Europe "NFL Europe"). The ELF played its first season in 2021,[[3]](#cite_note-Hamburger-3) and has 16 teams as of the [2025 season](/wiki/2025_European_League_of_Football_season "2025 European League of Football season"). Teams are located in [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") with plans to expand.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_League_of_Football&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The European League of Football was announced 4 November 2020 by [Patrick Esume](/wiki/Patrick_Esume "Patrick Esume"), former American Football coach and TV commentator, and sports manager Zeljko Karajica. Since 2018, Esume had criticized the [American Football Association of Germany](/wiki/American_Football_Association_of_Germany "American Football Association of Germany") (AFVD) for not using the fan potential in Germany, and tried to set up a professionalized structure for American football in Germany and the rest of Europe.

The announcement of the league was met with skepticism from AFVD and the [German Football League](/wiki/German_Football_League "German Football League") (GFL). AFVD president Robert Huber, said, "...we have experience over the last forty years in running leagues in Germany and in Europe and we have a pretty good insight into what the teams are capable of". However, Esume firmly believes that this league will succeed because "American football is experiencing a boom in Germany".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The first two new franchises to reveal their identity were the Ingolstadt Praetorians and Hanover-based team German Knights 1367, in early December 2020.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) They were followed by the Spanish team Gladiators Football, who also revealed they will play their games out of [Costa Daurada](/wiki/Costa_Daurada "Costa Daurada"), and will be coached by long-time [CFL](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League") coach [Adam Rita](/wiki/Adam_Rita "Adam Rita").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In March 2021, the league announced an agreement with the [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") to be able to use the names of former [NFL Europe](/wiki/NFL_Europe "NFL Europe") teams. On the same day, it was announced the franchises in Hamburg and Frankfurt would use the previous names of [Hamburg Sea Devils](/wiki/Hamburg_Sea_Devils_(ELF) "Hamburg Sea Devils (ELF)") and [Frankfurt Galaxy](/wiki/Frankfurt_Galaxy_(ELF) "Frankfurt Galaxy (ELF)").[[10]](#cite_note-10) The Sea Devils also introduced their new head coach, [Ted Daisher](/wiki/Ted_Daisher "Ted Daisher"), former special teams coach for multiple [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") teams.[[11]](#cite_note-11) It was then announced that the franchises in Ingolstadt and Hannover were not ready to launch in 2021; they were replaced with two new teams in Leipzig and Cologne. The franchises in Cologne, Berlin, and Barcelona would also take on the names from their [NFL Europe](/wiki/NFL_Europe "NFL Europe") counterparts.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### 2021 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_League_of_Football&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 2021 season")]

Main article: [2021 European League of Football season](/wiki/2021_European_League_of_Football_season "2021 European League of Football season")

On the 19 June 2021 at 6 p.m. the first kickoff of the ELF took place in [Wroclaw](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"). The host [Panthers](/wiki/Panthers_Wroc%C5%82aw "Panthers Wrocław") defeated the [Cologne Centurions](/wiki/Cologne_Centurions_(ELF) "Cologne Centurions (ELF)") 55–39. Frankfurt Galaxy won the South Division with only one defeat and defeated division rival Cologne Centurions in the semi-finals. The North Division was won by the Hamburg Sea Devils, who prevailed in the semi-finals against division opponent Panthers Wrocław. On September 26, 2021, the first final of the ELF took place before 22,000 in the Merkur Spiel-Arena in Düsseldorf. In a close game, Frankfurt Galaxy prevailed against Hamburg Sea Devils 32–30. A week later, an all-star game took place between the ELF all-stars and a selection of USA-based players.

In June 2021, the league announced a partnership agreement with the Brazilian Football Federation, [Brasil Futebol Americano](/wiki/Superliga_Nacional_de_Futebol_Americano "Superliga Nacional de Futebol Americano"), that will see exchange opportunities for selected players, coaches and officials as well as the possibility of organizing a match between the ELF and BFA champions in the future.[[13]](#cite_note-Brazil-13)

In July 2021, the league announced its inaugural All-Star Game, which was held one week after the end of the season, on 3 October ([German Unity Day](/wiki/German_Unity_Day "German Unity Day")), at [Friedrich-Ludwig-Jahn-Sportpark](/wiki/Friedrich-Ludwig-Jahn-Sportpark "Friedrich-Ludwig-Jahn-Sportpark") in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"). It will feature a selection of the best players from the ELF, playing against the [United States men's national American football team](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_American_football_team "United States men's national American football team").[[14]](#cite_note-14) Similar to the rules for rosters of individual teams, the all-star team was limited to a maximum of four Americans, only two of whom can be on the field at any given time.

### 2022 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_League_of_Football&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2022 season")]

Main article: [2022 European League of Football season](/wiki/2022_European_League_of_Football_season "2022 European League of Football season")

In September 2021, the first indications of new teams for the second season became known. 12 to 16 teams were announced for the 2022 season. On 12 September 2021, the [Vienna Vikings](/wiki/Vienna_Vikings "Vienna Vikings") from the [Austrian Football League](/wiki/Austrian_Football_League "Austrian Football League") announced their interest in joining the ELF in a press conference. The Austrian rival [Raiders Tirol](/wiki/Raiders_Tirol "Raiders Tirol") also confirmed entry into the league the day before the first ELF final. On the same day, [Rhein Fire](/wiki/Rhein_Fire_(ELF) "Rhein Fire (ELF)") from Düsseldorf was presented, taking over another name from the NFL Europe. On 15 October 2021 the league welcomed the [Istanbul Rams](/wiki/Istanbul_Rams "Istanbul Rams"), finalising the field of twelve teams for the 2022 season was completed.

In the 2022 season, the two Austrian expansion teams, Vienna Vikings and Raiders Tirol, were immediately among the strongest teams in the league. The Vikings won the Central Conference with 10 victories and 2 defeats; the Raiders Tirol qualified with 8–4 as the best conference second for the play-offs. Surprisingly the Barcelona Dragons won the Southern Conference with 8–4. Hamburg Sea Devils were the best team of the regular season and won the Northern Conference. After a 39–12 in the semi-final against the Dragons, the Vienna Vikings also won the Championship Game 2022 against the Sea Devils with 27–15.

### 2023 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_League_of_Football&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2023 season")]

Main article: [2023 European League of Football season](/wiki/2023_European_League_of_Football_season "2023 European League of Football season")

Three expansion teams for the 2023 season were already announced prior to the 2022 season: [Milano Seamen](/wiki/Milano_Seamen "Milano Seamen") from the [Italian Football League](/wiki/Italian_Football_League "Italian Football League"), Hungarian team [Fehérvár Enthroners](/wiki/Feh%C3%A9rv%C3%A1r_Enthroners "Fehérvár Enthroners") from the Austrian Football League Division 1, and the newly founded [Helvetic Guards](/wiki/Helvetic_Guards "Helvetic Guards"). In addition, on 3 August 2022 Munich and in September 2022 Paris and Prague were introduced as new franchises. On 16 December 2022 the Istanbul Rams withdrew from the league. The 2023 season thus started with 17 teams from nine countries. During the regular season, Leipzig Kings had to withdraw on 10 July 2023.

On the first weekend of the 2023 season, Rhein Fire set a new attendance record for the regular season in the Germany derby against Frankfurt Galaxy with 12665 spectators. The record was broken in the second week by the Hamburg Sea Devils, who played a game for the first time in [Volksparkstadion](/wiki/Volksparkstadion "Volksparkstadion") (capacity 57,000). 32,500 spectators came to the game against Rhein Fire. The regular season was dominated by Rhein Fire in the Western Conference and the Vienna Vikings in the Eastern Conference. Stuttgart Surge was able to secure the Central Conference under the new head coach [Jordan Neuman](/wiki/Jordan_Neuman "Jordan Neuman"). Frankfurt Galaxy, Panthers Wroclaw and, for the first time, Berlin Thunder, qualified for the playoffs. In the Wild Card Round, which was held for the first time, Frankfurt against Berlin and Stuttgart against Wroclaw used the home advantage. Rhein Fire also prevailed against Frankfurt Galaxy in the semi-finals in its own stadium. Opponent in the final was Stuttgart Surge, who were able to teach the Vienna Vikings the first home defeat in the ELF with the 40–33. Rhein Fire won the final.

### 2024 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_League_of_Football&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2024 season")]

Main article: [2024 European League of Football season](/wiki/2024_European_League_of_Football_season "2024 European League of Football season")

In the run-up to the 2023 season, Madrid was announced as expansion city. Leipzig Kings dropped out during the 2023 season. Helvetic Guards were replaced by a new Swiss team in April 2024.

The Madrid Bravos made an excellent start into the ELF. In week 2 they beat the reigning champion Rhein Fire. However, after the loss Fire won all other games and topped the Western Conference. The other conferences were also won by the previous year's winners: The Vienna Vikings in the East without loss, and Stuttgart Surge in the Central Conference with one loss against the Raiders Tirol. The Raiders failed to qualify after losing at Munich Ravens in the last regular season game. In the wild card round, Paris and Munich faced each other. In a game with ten leadership changes, the Musketeers were finally able to prevail. In the other wild card game, Rhein Fire took revenge for losing to the Bravos in the regular season. In the semi-finals, Fire met Stuttgart Surge in the replay of last year's final. Surge looked like the winner for a long time, but Rhein Fire was able to equalize shortly before the end and finally prevail in extra time. The Vienna Vikings prevailed with 47-31 against Paris. The championship game was played in the [Arena AufSchalke](/wiki/Arena_AufSchalke "Arena AufSchalke") in [Gelsenkirchen](/wiki/Gelsenkirchen "Gelsenkirchen"). In the game between the champions of the two previous seasons, Rhein Fire had the upper hand.

### 2025 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_League_of_Football&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2025 season")]

Main article: [2025 European League of Football season](/wiki/2025_European_League_of_Football_season "2025 European League of Football season")

The [Nordic Storm](/wiki/Nordic_Storm "Nordic Storm"), representing Copenhagen in Denmark and Malmö in Sweden, joined the league for the 2025 season. The Barcelona Dragons folded, whereas the [Milano Seamen](/wiki/Milano_Seamen "Milano Seamen") are on hiatus. With QB Jadrian Clark and RB Glen Toonga, Nordic Storm signed two key players from champion Rhein Fire. Former Fire OC Andrew Weidinger joined [Madrid Bravos](/wiki/Madrid_Bravos "Madrid Bravos") as HC. [Jack Del Rio](/wiki/Jack_Del_Rio "Jack Del Rio") joined the [Paris Musketeers](/wiki/Paris_Musketeers "Paris Musketeers") as HC. [DJ Irons](/wiki/DJ_Irons "DJ Irons") joined [Wroclaw Panthers](/wiki/Wroclaw_Panthers "Wroclaw Panthers") as their QB for the 2025 season as they struggled with QBs last year. The ELF struck a deal with [DAZN](/wiki/DAZN "DAZN") to broadcast all their games with their Game Pass.

[Stuttgart Surge](/wiki/Stuttgart_Surge "Stuttgart Surge") achieved their first title ever after defeating [Vienna Vikings](/wiki/Vienna_Vikings "Vienna Vikings") 24–17 in the final.

### 2026 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_League_of_Football&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2026 season")]

Main article: [2026 European League of Football season](/w/index.php?title=2026_European_League_of_Football_season&action=edit&redlink=1 "2026 European League of Football season (page does not exist)")

At the end of the season, eleven teams — [Berlin Thunder](/wiki/Berlin_Thunder_(ELF) "Berlin Thunder (ELF)"), [Rhein Fire](/wiki/Rhein_Fire_(ELF) "Rhein Fire (ELF)"), [Nordic Storm](/wiki/Nordic_Storm "Nordic Storm"), [Frankfurt Galaxy](/wiki/Frankfurt_Galaxy_(ELF) "Frankfurt Galaxy (ELF)"), [Paris Musketeers](/wiki/Paris_Musketeers "Paris Musketeers"), [Stuttgart Surge](/wiki/Stuttgart_Surge "Stuttgart Surge"), [Madrid Bravos](/wiki/Madrid_Bravos "Madrid Bravos"), [Raiders Tirol](/wiki/Raiders_Tirol "Raiders Tirol"), [Panthers Wrocław](/wiki/Panthers_Wroc%C5%82aw "Panthers Wrocław"), [Prague Lions](/wiki/Prague_Lions "Prague Lions"), and [Vienna Vikings](/wiki/Vienna_Vikings "Vienna Vikings") — left the ELF and established a new league called the *[European Football Alliance](/wiki/European_Football_Alliance "European Football Alliance")* (EFA).[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) The ELF later announced that they had eleven teams "under valid contract" for the 2026 season and were in "intensive discussions" with multiple prospective partners and "remains committed to open dialogue with all current franchises, with the clear goal of building a shared foundation for the further development of the league and of American football across Europe".[[18]](#cite_note-18)

On November 26, 2025, the ELF and EFA announced that the two competitions would be reunifying with a number of administrative and commercial changes ahead of the 2026 season.[[19]](#cite_note-19) However, on January 15, 2026, EFA terminated the cooperation agreement due to ELF missing financial stability and transparency.[[20]](#cite_note-20) EFA will go forward with their initial plan to establish an independent league. In parallel, four teams split from the EFL to the newly created [American Football League Europe](/wiki/American_Football_League_Europe "American Football League Europe").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In February 2026, the ELF announced that they were entering preliminary self-administration proceedings under German insolvency law. According to the ELF’s statement, this comes as a result of anticipated investor funding not materializing. All operations, including games, continue during this process.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Goals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_League_of_Football&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Goals")]

The ELF has said it hopes to bring back the excitement around American football that existed in the era of NFL Europe. Unlike NFL Europe, whose team rosters consisted of mostly American players, there will be restrictions to the number of international players allowed on rosters. There will be a bigger focus given to homegrown players, with the goal to develop the best of these players into NFL prospects. One way they plan on doing this is by creating an "ELF Academy". While ELF franchises will not have "youth teams", CEO Zeljko Karajica says that the academy will "...give players the opportunity to make the next step", which would be the skills to succeed in the ELF and compete for a spot on an NFL team.

## Rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_League_of_Football&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Rules")]

The ELF plays with modified [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") rules, except for overtime, where it uses the [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association")'s [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football") rules. Unlike most European sports leagues, (including European American football leagues) a game that is still tied at the end of regulation during the regular season goes to [overtime](/wiki/Overtime_(sports) "Overtime (sports)"). However, no overtime was actually played in the [inaugural 2021 season](/wiki/2021_European_League_of_Football_season "2021 European League of Football season") as all games had a winner after regulation. During the regular season of the inaugural 2021 season, the ELF did not have [instant replay](/wiki/Instant_replay "Instant replay") or other forms of [replay review in gridiron football](/wiki/Replay_review_in_gridiron_football "Replay review in gridiron football") (such as the coach's challenge); however, this feature was introduced for the 2021 ELF Bowl as well as for the upcoming season.

On 30 November 2021, the official Twitter account of the European League of Football announced a new kickoff rule. The new rule sees the kicking team's players, except the kicker, line up on its opponent's 35-yard line, while the receiving team's players, except the returner, line up on their own 30-yard line. Both teams are only allowed to move when the ball has either been touched by the returner or three seconds after the ball has touched the ground. This rule is nearly identical to the kickoff rule used by the 2020 iteration of the [XFL](/wiki/XFL_(2020) "XFL (2020)") and later by the NFL. Only the requirement, that the ball must be kicked between the receiving team's 20-yard line and endzone, is, at this time, not a part of the rule. The main cited reason for the rule change is player safety.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Officiating

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_League_of_Football&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Officiating")]

[Officiating](/wiki/Official_(gridiron_football) "Official (gridiron football)") in the ELF is comparable to the NFL. Head of officiation since the first season in 2021 is former [German Football League](/wiki/German_Football_League "German Football League") official Kurt Paulus. In this role, he is in charge of training and organizing the officiating crews for individual game days. Furthermore, the head of officiating and its deputies are consulting the commissioner and Competition Committee for rule changes and new rules.[[24]](#cite_note-FootBowl#6-24)

Officiating crews consist of seven on-field officials who stay together for a season. During a game, they are connected via intercom and English-language on-field dialogue. In the 2022 season, the referees came from 16 different European countries. With the exception of the GFL and its governing body, referees officiating in the ELF are allowed to participate in national leagues during the ELF offseason.[[24]](#cite_note-FootBowl#6-24) Officials in the ELF do not wear numbers on their jerseys.

## Salary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_League_of_Football&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Salary")]

Rosters consist of a maximum of 53 players, plus another 12 on the practice squad as of 2023. Because homegrown players are one of the main focus points of the league, there are limitations on the number of foreigners. Each roster can only have a maximum of four U.S., Canadian, Mexican, or Japanese players (A-players), and a maximum of ten other foreign players for the 2021 season,[[3]](#cite_note-Hamburger-3) which was then reduced to eight players for the [2022 season](/wiki/2022_European_League_of_Football_season "2022 European League of Football season") and six players for the [2023 season](/wiki/2023_European_League_of_Football_season "2023 European League of Football season").[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) However, the [Bosman ruling](/wiki/Bosman_ruling "Bosman ruling") prevents discrimination against EU citizens on EU-based sports teams and leagues. Brazilian players did not count towards the import quota due to the league's partnership with [Liga BFA](/wiki/Superliga_Nacional_de_Futebol_Americano "Superliga Nacional de Futebol Americano") in the 2021 season.[[13]](#cite_note-Brazil-13)

The league has a [salary cap](/wiki/Salary_cap "Salary cap") for all franchises which is divided into three salary groups for its players. Up to eight players—including the four A-import spots—are paid a full-time salary. For American import players, a franchise can pay a salary that can range from €600 (around $700) to €3,000 ($3,500) per month.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The second tier consists of additional four transitional players (international or homegrown) with a part-time salary. Every other member of the roster is in the homegrown salary group with [marginal employment](/wiki/Marginal_employment "Marginal employment") and a monthly income ranging from €100 to €450. All players under contract receive health insurance and participate in state pension insurance. Further benefits such as housing and meals during the season have to be negotiated individually.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

European League of Football (Europe)

Locations of teams competing in the 2025 ELF season.

* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Blue_pog.svg/20px-Blue_pog.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Blue_pog.svg) North Division
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Green_pog.svg/20px-Green_pog.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Green_pog.svg) West Division
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Red_pog.svg/20px-Red_pog.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Red_pog.svg) South Division
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Yellow_pog.svg/20px-Yellow_pog.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Yellow_pog.svg) East Division

For the 2025 season, the European League of Football has 16 teams in four divisions across 10 countries: 7 in Germany, 2 in Austria, one each in the Czech Republic, Denmark, France, Hungary, Spain, Poland, and Switzerland. The ELF began with 8 teams in its [inaugural season](/wiki/2021_European_League_of_Football_season "2021 European League of Football season") in 2021. This included the [Wroclaw Panthers](/wiki/Wroclaw_Panthers "Wroclaw Panthers") from the [Polish American Football League](/wiki/Polish_American_Football_League "Polish American Football League"), and seven new franchises: the [Berlin Thunder](/wiki/Berlin_Thunder_(ELF) "Berlin Thunder (ELF)"), [Cologne Centurions](/wiki/Cologne_Centurions_(ELF) "Cologne Centurions (ELF)"), [Frankfurt Galaxy](/wiki/Frankfurt_Galaxy_(ELF) "Frankfurt Galaxy (ELF)"), [Hamburg Sea Devils](/wiki/Hamburg_Sea_Devils_(ELF) "Hamburg Sea Devils (ELF)"), [Leipzig Kings](/wiki/Leipzig_Kings "Leipzig Kings") and [Stuttgart Surge](/wiki/Stuttgart_Surge "Stuttgart Surge") in Germany, and [Barcelona Dragons](/wiki/Barcelona_Dragons_(ELF) "Barcelona Dragons (ELF)") in Spain. The goal of the league is to expand to at least 24 teams from 10 countries in the future. As early as 2021, Austria, France, and England were identified as markets interested in expansion franchises, and the [London Warriors](/wiki/London_Warriors "London Warriors") in particular expressed an interest in joining the league.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The [Vienna Vikings](/wiki/Vienna_Vikings "Vienna Vikings") and [Raiders Tirol](/wiki/Raiders_Tirol "Raiders Tirol") from the [Austrian Football League](/wiki/Austrian_Football_League "Austrian Football League"), and the [Istanbul Rams](/wiki/Istanbul_Rams "Istanbul Rams") from the [Turkish Gridiron Football First League](/wiki/Turkish_Gridiron_Football_First_League "Turkish Gridiron Football First League") joined in the [2022 season](/wiki/2022_European_League_of_Football_season "2022 European League of Football season"), though the Rams dropped out after the season's conclusion.[[30]](#cite_note-rams-30) A reformed [Rhein Fire](/wiki/Rhein_Fire_(ELF) "Rhein Fire (ELF)") inspired by the [historical team](/wiki/Rhein_Fire_(NFL_Europe) "Rhein Fire (NFL Europe)") from [NFL Europe](/wiki/NFL_Europe "NFL Europe") also joined during the 2022 season after much speculation and anticipation from fans throughout the previous season.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) Though a feud with the [Italian Football League](/wiki/Italian_Football_League "Italian Football League") initially precluded an Italian expansion franchise, an eventual agreement allowed the [Milano Seamen](/wiki/Milano_Seamen "Milano Seamen") to join in the [2023 season](/wiki/2023_European_League_of_Football_season "2023 European League of Football season"),[[34]](#cite_note-34) which also saw the [Fehérvár Enthroners](/wiki/Feh%C3%A9rv%C3%A1r_Enthroners "Fehérvár Enthroners") from the [Hungarian Football League](/wiki/Hungarian_American_Football_League "Hungarian American Football League") and [Prague Lions](/wiki/Prague_Lions "Prague Lions") from the [Czech League of American Football](/wiki/Czech_League_of_American_Football "Czech League of American Football") join, along with three new franchises: the [Helvetic Guards](/wiki/Helvetic_Guards "Helvetic Guards") from Switzerland, [Munich Ravens](/wiki/Munich_Ravens "Munich Ravens") from Germany,[[35]](#cite_note-35) and [Paris Musketeers](/wiki/Paris_Musketeers "Paris Musketeers") from France. The Kings were suspended amid the 2023 season, the Guards were replaced by the [Helvetic Mercenaries](/wiki/Helvetic_Mercenaries "Helvetic Mercenaries") in April 2024, while the [Madrid Bravos](/wiki/Madrid_Bravos "Madrid Bravos") joined in 2024. [Nordic Storm](/wiki/Nordic_Storm "Nordic Storm") from [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") will be the newest addition for the 2025 season.

## Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_League_of_Football&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

For the inaugural season, the league signed a deal to have 13 games, including the playoffs and championship game, broadcast live in Germany, Austria and Switzerland on [ProSieben Maxx](/wiki/ProSieben_Maxx "ProSieben Maxx"), with all other games streamed live on ran.de and More Than Sports TV.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) The number of games available on TV increased in 2023, with 30 games shown live on [ProSieben](/wiki/ProSieben "ProSieben") channels, including the Championship Game on their primary channel.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

All games are also available worldwide, on the league's website, service originally provided by British company [StreamAMG](/wiki/PA_Media "PA Media"), and, from 2023, by [Endeavor Streaming](/wiki/Endeavor_Streaming "Endeavor Streaming").[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)

On 30 January 2025, ELF announced an agreement with [DAZN](/wiki/DAZN "DAZN") for broadcasting all the league games until 2032.[[1]](#cite_note-DAZN-1)

raw_content:
[![European Leagues](https://europeanleagues.com/wp-content/uploads/european_leagues_logo_horizontal.png)](https://europeanleagues.com/)

# European Leagues

[Members Login](/login/)

# European Leagues’ main goal is to enhance and protect competitive balance in league competitions

## Who We Are

The European Leagues – the Association of European Professional Football Leagues – is the voice of Professional Football Leagues across Europe on all matters of common interest. The European Leagues represent the vast majority of club football employers in the European social dialogue for the professional football sector.

The European Leagues, which is an association governed by Swiss law founded in 2005 and based in Nyon Switzerland, traces its origins back to the founding in 1997 of the EUPPFL (Association of European Union Premier Professional Football Leagues).

Originally founded by 14 members: Austria, Belgium, Denmark, England, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Italy, Netherlands, Portugal, Scotland, Spain and Sweden. The European Leagues now counts 40 professional football leagues and associations of clubs from 34 countries, representing more than 1170 clubs from all over Europe.

## European Leagues Vision

To be the leading representative of professional league football in Europe on all matters of common interest.

## European Leagues Mission

Our mission is to enhance and protect the national competitions run by the domestic leagues, so they can help their member clubs perform at the highest sporting level in a sustainable and competitively balanced environment.

To do so the European Leagues works in cooperation with the football governing bodies (FIFA, UEFA and National Associations) and public authorities across Europe through an active dialogue, including other relevant stakeholders in football.

![](https://europeanleagues.com/wp-content/uploads/SEMINARIO-44-scaled.jpg)

MILANO 21/22.10.2021 SEDE LEGA CALCIO/A.G.EUROPEAN LEAGUES /ph Fabio Bozzani/SPORT/CALCIO/Nella foto

# European Leagues Structure

The European Leagues is composed of a General Assembly (legislative body), a President and Vice President, the Board of Directors (executive body), the Strategic Committees (advisory bodies) and the European Leagues Administration (administrative body).

* [General Assembly](#general_assembly-1)
* [President](#management-board)
* [Board of Directors](#board-of-directors)
* [Strategic Committees](#strategic-committees)
* [Administration](#administration)

# General Assembly

The General Assembly brings together all European Leagues Member Leagues and Associate Members. It is the legislative body of the European Leagues, which determines the general policies of the Association and can give all powers and authority to the Board of Directors.

The General Assembly meets at least twice a year.

# European Leagues President & Board of Directors

## The President:

Claudius Schäfer, CEO of the Swiss Football League, was formally appointed President of the European Leagues by the 50th General Assembly held on March 6, 2025 in Frankfurt.

## The Board of Directors:

The European Leagues Board of Directors is the executive body of the Association and is entrusted with the overall control and supervision of the same. It consists of thirteen members, each of them elected by the General Assembly for a term of 4 years. European Leagues Board of Directors for the 2025-2029 cycle is as follows:
