39 References 41 5 1 Introduction 1.1 Research Motivation and Objectives The analysis of professional football has undergone a marked evolution with the advent of high-resolution event and tracking data. Traditional statistics such as goals scored or shots on target offer limited insight into underlying performance, whereas advanced metrics like expected goals (xG) quantify the quality of scoring opportunities and have been shown to outperform raw counts in predicting match outcomes Fern´ andez et al. (2018) and MacKenzie and Cushion (2012). Yet xG remains only one aspect of performance. Teams differ markedly in their defensive resilience, transition efficiency, disciplinary control and finishing quality, and these dimensions can interact in non-linear ways that simple additive models fail to capture.
To accommodate such complexity we turn to quantile regression, first formalised by Koenker and Bassett (1978) which characterises the entire conditional distribution of match outcomes rather than just the mean. Gelman et al. (2013) and Baio and Blan-giardo (2010) extended this with Bayesian hierarchical modelling, allowing pooling of information across teams, thereby improving estimates for clubs with fewer observa-tions while still permitting team-specific effects.
Furthermore, Barry and Hartigan (1993) demonstrated that change-point techniques have the capacity to reveal thresh-old values—points at which minor enhancements in performance indicators result in significant increases in the probability of success.
Despite these advances, no study to date combines quantile regression, hierarchical partial pooling and team-specific change-points within a single framework that incorpo-rates multiple key performance indicators (beyond xG). Such an integrated approach would not only enhance predictive performance but also deliver clear, team-tailored targets that are directly interpretable by practitioners.
This thesis brings these strands together in a unified, hierarchical framework.
Specifically, we investigate: 1. Team-specific thresholds for high-confidence wins: How do 90th percentile change-points for xG difference vary across Premier League teams, and what is the extent of that variability?
6 2. Incremental contributions of multiple KPIs: Beyond xG difference, which additional key performance indicators, namely pos-session share, defensive actions, chance creation, disciplinary intensity, directness of play, and finishing efficiency, significantly influence win probability at the 90th percentile, and how do their interactions modify these effects?
3. Comparative predictive performance: Does a Bayesian hierarchical quantile regression model with team-specific change-points outperform: (a) a quantile regression without change-points, and (b) a non-hierarchical change-point model, both in terms of predictive accuracy and the clarity of actionable insights?
By addressing these questions, this study aims to enhance decision-making through a careful integration of hierarchical pooling, quantile regression and change-point anal-ysis, and to offer practical, team-level benchmarks that analysts and coaches can adapt to support more nuanced tactical planning.
1.2 Context: Predicting English Premier League Outcomes The English Premier League (EPL) is widely regarded as one of the most competi-tive and financially significant football competitions in the world. With twenty teams participating in thirty-eight matches each season, the league generates a substantial volume of event-level data, including shots, passes, tackles, and tracking information that allows detailed analysis of performance patterns. The ability to predict the out-comes of EPL matches has historically attracted interest from coaches, analysts, and commercial stakeholders for two primary reasons: firstly, it facilitates strategic plan-ning, and secondly, it impacts betting markets.
Early efforts to forecast results relied on simple aggregate statistics such as goals scored, shots on target, or home/away win percentages (Dixon & Coles, 2002; Maher, 1982). More recently, expected-goals (xG) models have become the standard baseline, as they estimate the probability of each shot becoming a goal based on factors like shot location and type Fern´ andez et al. (2018) and MacKenzie and Cushion (2012). Lucey 7 et al. (2014) demonstrate that xG-based logistic regressions generally achieve a success rate of approximately 65–70 percent in predicting the occurrence of wins or losses.
Beyond xG, machine-learning methods such as random forests, gradient boosting, and neural networks have been applied to richer feature sets, incorporating possession metrics, defensive actions, and player-tracking variables (Bunker & Thabtah, 2019; De-croos et al., 2019). While these models can marginally improve predictive performance, they frequently lack interpretability: they identify which variables are important, but not the precise values at which win probability jumps.
At the same time, hierarchical Bayesian models have been used to pool information across teams or seasons, controlling for small-sample noise and enabling team-level comparisons (Baio & Blangiardo, 2010). However, few such models have focused on conditional quantiles, which are key to understanding “high-confidence” winning sce-narios, nor on estimating abrupt thresholds in predictor-outcome relationships.
In this context, the objective of our work is to build on these strands by: • retaining the predictive strength of xG and related KPIs; • introducing quantile regression to target extreme win probabilities; • applying team-specific change-point detection to reveal the exact KPI values at which win chances increase sharply; and • using a hierarchical framework to borrow strength across teams while still allow-ing individual variation.
By situating these methods within the particular data environment of the EPL, where abundant annotations in match records and consistent competitive structures serve to facilitate robust estimation, the present study provides a realistic evaluation of advanced modelling techniques and actionable insights with customised applicability at the level of individual clubs.
1.3 Contributions and Originality The present thesis makes several original contributions at the intersection of sports analytics, Bayesian statistics, and decision-support practice: 8 1. Unified hierarchical quantile change-point framework Although previous studies have employed quantile regression Koenker and Bas-sett (1978) or hierarchical Bayesian pooling Gelman et al. (2013), and certain studies have investigated change-point detection in time series Chib (1998), no studies have hitherto integrated all three into a unified model for predicting the outcomes of football matches. This integration is important because combining quantile regression, hierarchical pooling and team-specific change-point detection in a single framework lets us model the full distribution of win probabilities ,in-cluding extreme outcomes, borrow information across clubs and pinpoint KPI thresholds, thereby converting raw match statistics into team-specific, actionable guidance for coaches and analysts.
The proposed framework has been developed to achieve three primary objectives: firstly, to estimate the τ = 0.90 conditional quantile of win probability; secondly, to pool information across twenty EPL teams via partial pooling; and thirdly, to detect team-specific thresholds in key predictors.
2. Team-specific, actionable KPI thresholds The majority of performance-modelling research reports global effect sizes or fea-ture importances (Bunker & Thabtah, 2019), yet practitioners require concrete benchmarks (e.g., “if xGDiff > 1.3, win odds rise sharply”). By estimating pos-terior distributions for each team’s change-point, we deliver credible intervals for KPI thresholds, thereby translating statistical inference into precise, team-level targets for coaching, training, and recruitment.
3. Quantile-focused evaluation of extreme outcomes Conventional outcome models place emphasis on overall accuracy or mean out-comes (e.g., AUC, RMSE), which serve to obscure performance in scenarios where high confidence is held. The targeting of the 90th percentile win probability is directly aligned with managerial interests in “matches we expect to win” and provides a superior fit to the tail of the distribution.
4. Empirical validation on a data-rich competition The application of the framework to four seasons of EPL data (approximately 1,500 matches) leverages one of the world’s most extensively tracked leagues.
This methodology facilitates the estimation of hierarchical effects and change-points under real-world data conditions, while controlling for competition format and event-collection consistency.
9 5. Comparative assessment of model complexity vs. gain In this study, we compare three models: (i) a hierarchical quantile-only model, (ii) a non-hierarchical change-point model, and (iii) the full hierarchical change-point model. The present study employs out-of-sample quantile loss to quantify the trade-off between added complexity and predictive improvement, thereby informing practitioners about the marginal benefit of each modelling layer.
Collectively, these contributions enhance the methodological repertoire for sports analytics and augment the practical capabilities of coaches and analysts in establishing data-driven performance objectives. To the best of our knowledge, this constitutes the inaugural implementation of Bayesian hierarchical quantile regression with team-specific change-point detection in top-tier football.
The remainder of this manuscript is organised as follows. Section 2 reviews the perti-nent literature on statistical forecasting in sport, with particular emphasis on quantile methods and change-point analysis. Section 3 introduces the English Premier League dataset, defines the key performance indicators, and details the Bayesian quantile re-gression framework incorporating team-specific threshold effects. Section 4 presents the empirical findings, including predictive-accuracy comparisons, estimated thresh-olds, and their practical implications for analysts. Section 5 concludes by summaris-ing the principal contributions, acknowledging limitations, and outlining directions for future research.
2 Literature review 2.1 Evolution of Outcome Models Early efforts to predict football scores treated each team’s goals as arising indepen-dently from simple count processes, most notably in Maher (1982)’s pioneering work.
In this formulation, a team’s attacking and defensive qualities determine its expected goal tally, with the result adjusted for home advantage. Whilst the independent-goals approach is successful in capturing overall scoring tendencies, it systematically misrep-resents the frequency of low-scoring draws.
To address this issue, Dixon and Coles (2002) proposed a minor adjustment, which 10 links the two goal counts when both teams score a low number of goals. This ad-justment brings modelled probabilities for 0–0, 1–0 and similar outcomes into closer agreement with observed data, markedly improving forecast accuracy without sacrific-ing interpretability.
Subsequent refinements have extended these ideas by allowing for extra variation in goal counts or incorporating up-to-date information on team form. However, the core independent-goals plus low-scoring correction remains the benchmark against which newer quantile- and change-point models are compared.
2.2 Quantile Regression in Sports Analytics Quantile regression (QR), as formalised by Koenker and Bassett (1978), generalises the classical multiple linear regression framework by estimating conditional quantiles, rather than the conditional mean, which is typically fitted via ordinary least-squares es-timation. This framework facilitates a more detailed analysis of covariate effects across the outcome distribution. QR is particularly robust to outliers, naturally accommo-dates heteroscedasticity, and operates without requiring strict assumptions about the error distribution.
Ribeiro et al. (2025) employed a Bayesian modeling framework to predict football match outcomes using data from the 2022 Brazilian Championship Series A. Their approach integrated variations of Poisson regression models, accounting for features like overdispersion, hierarchy, and informative priors derived from previous seasons. The study demonstrated that Bayesian models—particularly dynamic Poisson models with zero inflation—provided robust and interpretable estimates, even in the presence of data irregularities such as unexpected results and outliers. The predictive performance and model fit remained consistent across different model specifications, underscoring the robustness of Bayesian regression approaches without presenting a clear advantage over simpler frequentist methods in this particular context.
Beyond football, QR has demonstrated significant utility in other sports domains.
Bornn et al. (2016) utilised Bayesian quantile regression to analyse cricket batsmen’s score distributions.
By modelling the conditional quantiles, they revealed nuanced differences in player risk profiles that might be obscured by mean-based models.
11 In a more applied context, Dmochowski et al. (2023) explored NFL point-spread betting and demonstrated how focusing on specific quantiles of outcomes can be utilised for profitable strategies, thereby emphasising the decision-theoretic benefits of QR.
Despite these varied applications, there is limited use of quantile regression in pre-dictive modelling of match outcomes in elite-level football. The present research seeks to address this gap by focusing on the 90th percentile (τ = 0.90) of win probability in the English Premier League.
By modelling the upper quantile, we target high-confidence scenarios, which are crucial for decision-makers such as coaches and ana-lysts. Furthermore, we leverage QR’s capacity to expose threshold effects that may be overlooked by mean-based models.
2.3 Performance indicators for match-outcome models This subsection reviews the empirical support for each performance indicator later used in our model. Drawing on recent work from the Premier League, Champions League and other top-tier competitions, it groups findings under five themes: offensive efficiency, possession and progression, defensive solidity, disciplinary impact, and key interactions.
2.3.1 Attacking-efficiency metrics The proliferation of event and tracking-based data has shifted performance analysis away from crude tallies such as shots or goals towards indicators that explicitly weight chance quality. Expected goals (xG) models, which combine shot location, angle and contextual features, now represent the benchmark offensive KPI. In a cross-league study covering the EPL, La Liga, Bundesliga, Serie A and Ligue 1, Mead et al. (2023) showed that team-level xG improved next-match result accuracy from roughly 0.47 (shots/goals baseline) to 0.52–0.54 and reduced mean-squared-error for six-match goal-ratio fore-casts by about one-third (e.g., 0.2925 →0.2013 in the Premier League) rather than by 50 per cent as sometimes claimed. These findings confirm that xG captures underlying dominance more reliably than raw volume measures.
While xG gauges the probability of scoring, the execution of chances remains criti-cal. A discriminant-function analysis of the 2019 AFCON(Kubayi & Larkin, 2022) 12 tournament classified 78.9 per cent of matches correctly; the single most powerful sep-arator of winners and losers was shots on target (structure coefficient = 1.22), eclipsing total-shot count. Similar patterns emerge in league play: in the 2020–21 Greek Super League(Stafylidis, Mandroukas, Michailidis, Vardakis, et al., 2024) each additional shot on target increased the odds of winning by 69 per cent (OR = 1.693) after controlling for other factors.
A recent refinement is expected goals on target (xGOT), which incorporates post-shot trajectory and goalkeeper positioning. In Spain’s 2023/24 season (Ruiz-de-Alarc´ on-Quintero & De-la-Cruz-Torres, 2024), xGOT correlated almost perfectly with goals per match in La Liga (R² = 0.925) and Liga F (R² = 0.957), outperforming both xG and possession-value metrics. This suggests that modelling the quality of the strike adds explanatory power where finishing skill or goalkeeping prowess matter.
Collectively, the evidence indicates three complementary strands for quantifying attacking efficiency: 1.Chance creation quality (xG) 2.Finishing accuracy (shots on target and xGOT) 3. Context-sensitive execution (e.g., counter-attack shots, set-piece efficiency, ex-amined in §2.3.2).
By integrating these strands our subsequent hierarchical quantile-regression frame-work seeks to trace how offensive dominance translates into results without overstating effect sizes observed in individual studies.
2.3.2 Possession- and progression-based indicators Recent work separates ball control (how long a team retains possession) from ball progression (how effectively it moves the ball into dangerous areas). In keeping with this distinction we retain three covariates: PossessionDiff, DirectnessDiff and AttackingActionsDiff, while recognising that the empirical support for each is un-even.
13 (i) Possession share (PossessionDiff) Farias et al. (2020) report that, across five UEFA Champions-League seasons, the team holding the larger possession share won 49 % of matches and lost 29 %; the suc-cess rate exceeded 70 % when the possession gap was greater than 15 percentage points.
They also stress, however, that possession is “only one of many variables” influencing results. In a 12 289-match cross-continental study, Jacinto et al. (2024) retained posses-sion percentage for South-American leagues but excluded it from their UEFA logistic model because of multicollinearity with other attacking metrics. These mixed findings justify keeping PossessionDiff as a broad indicator of territorial control while allow-ing the hierarchical model to shrink its impact if stronger, related predictors absorb the signal.
(ii) Directness of attack (DirectnessDiff) Using tracking data from the 2018 and 2022 FIFA World Cups, Taha and Orlov (2025) showed that a directness ratio (straight-line progress towards goal divided by total ball path) was the only contextual variable that remained a positive, significant predictor of a possession ending in a goal after team effects were accounted for. Because event feeds do not provide full ball-path coordinates, we approximate the same concept with each team’s shots-on-target per 100 touches and define DirectnessDiff as the home-minus-away gap in that percentage (see Section 3.1.3 for the exact calculation).
The World-Cup finding supports the underlying idea that rapid, forward-oriented at-tacks are more likely to yield goals, even though our operational measure is a proxy derived from available data.
(iii) Penetrative actions (AttackingActionsDiff) Goes et al. (2019) analysed Dutch Eredivisie tracking data and found that posses-sions containing at least one line-breaking pass or carry were more than twice as likely to finish with a shot; adding those events to a random-forest classifier lifted match-result accuracy to 64 %. Jacinto et al. (2024) further showed that entries into the opponent’s final third carried the strongest odds ratio among territorial variables in both CONMEBOL and UEFA samples. To capture the same notion with our event data, AttackingActionsDiff is defined as the difference in crosses + touches + cor-ners between the two teams, giving a high-volume indicator of purposeful supply into dangerous areas.
14 Summary Possession share, the speed with which possession is turned into on-target shots, and the rate of attacking supply actions capture overlapping but distinct as-pects of territorial dominance.
Including PossessionDiff, DirectnessDiff and AttackingActionsDiff allows the Bayesian hierarchical quantile-regression model to test whether each adds unique predictive value; where collinearity emerges (as in Jac-into et al.’s UEFA sample) the hierarchical shrinkage prior will naturally down-weight the redundant term rather than requiring manual feature removal.
2.3.3 Defensive solidity Recent multi-league work shows that regaining possession, especially through recoveries and interceptions, has a measurable, positive association with winning, whereas raw tackle volume offers at best an inconsistent signal.
Recoveries and interceptions In a 12 289-match logistic analysis of the five top CONMEBOL and UEFA leagues, Jacinto et al. (2024) reported that ball recoveries and interceptions were significant positive predictors of victory in both confederations (odds ratios ≈1.3–2.0). The authors noted that these effects remained after other technical variables in the model, although possession percentage was removed from the UEFA subset because of multicollinearity.
Tackling and fouling In the 2020–21 Greek Super League, Stafylidis, Mandroukas, Michailidis, and Metaxas (2024) found that every additional free-kick conceded-serving as a proxy for mistimed or overly aggressive challenges-significantly reduced a team’s likelihood of victory (B ≈−0.21, p < 0.001), underscoring the premium placed on disciplined rather than simply frequent defensive interventions.
Tournament evidence At the 2019 Africa Cup of Nations, winning teams did not execute more tackles than losers, but losing sides committed more fouls and accrued more yellow cards, reinforcing the importance of disciplined rather than merely aggres-sive defending (Kubayi & Larkin, 2022).
15 Implication for the predictor set Given the consistent signal carried by recoveries and interceptions, the model retains DefActionsDiff, the home-minus-away total of tackles + interceptions + recoveries. Tackles remain for completeness, but the hierar-chical shrinkage prior allows the data to down-weight them if they add little once the more informative recovery-based actions are included.
2.3.4 Disciplinary impact Playing a man short, or even the threat of doing so, can alter both the tactical balance and the expected score line. Recent large-sample evidence quantifies just how costly cards can be.
Red cards Analysing 1,826 matches from the Premier League, La Liga, Serie A, the Bundesliga and Ligue 1 (2013–2019), Badiella et al. (2023) estimated a mixed-effects Poisson model that switches goal rates the moment a dismissal occurs. A single red card reduced the penalised team’s scoring intensity by roughly one-quarter and raised the opponent’s by a similar margin, translating into a swing of about 0.6 expected goals over the remainder of the match. The effect was immediate and persisted to full-time.
Yellow cards The same study found that yellow cards had a smaller, short-lived impact: goal-scoring intensity dipped by 6–8 % for the cautioned side in the ten minutes following the booking, then returned to baseline. Nevertheless, cumulative yellows increase the probability of a subsequent dismissal and therefore retain strategic relevance.
Implications for modelling Because numerical inferiority and disciplinary pressure can shift win expectancy within minutes, we retain CardIntensityDiff, the net difference in red plus yellow card counts at full time, as a dynamic contextual covariate.
Its inclusion lets the hierarchical quantile framework capture matches where one side’s advantage is driven less by technical dominance than by disciplinary lapses.
16 2.3.5 Summary and implications Across the strands reviewed, a coherent picture emerges. Quantitative studies agree that quality-adjusted attacking metrics, notably expected-goals difference and finishing efficiency, show the strongest and most stable association with match success (Jacinto et al., 2024; Mead et al., 2023). Selective ball progression also matters: possession advantages translate into wins chiefly when the ball is moved forward quickly and into the final third (Farias et al., 2020; Taha & Orlov, 2025). On the defensive side, ball recoveries and interceptions outperform raw tackle counts as predictors of victory, underscoring the importance of disciplined ball-winning (Jacinto et al., 2024; Stafylidis, Mandroukas, Michailidis, & Metaxas, 2024). Finally, disciplinary events, especially red cards, impose immediate sizeable swings in expected goals and consistently tilt results (Badiella et al., 2023; Kubayi & Larkin, 2022) . These findings have been replicated in Europe’s top five leagues, continental competitions and international tournaments, suggesting a degree of cross-context robustness.
2.4 Research gap and study aims The review in Chapters 2.1–2.3 yields three firm conclusions. First, mean-based out-come models, from early Poisson approaches (Dixon & Coles, 2002; Maher, 1982) to modern machine-learning classifiers, are now complemented by distribution-wide meth-ods such as quantile regression, which capture the whole range of possible results.
Second, four performance domains have the strongest empirical links to success: (i) quality-adjusted attacking metrics (expected-goals difference, post-shot xG and finishing efficiency), (ii) purposeful progression (entries into the final third, progres-sive or line-breaking passes), (iii) disciplined ball recovery (recoveries and interceptions rather than raw tackle counts), and (iv) disciplinary events, with red cards producing the clearest negative swing.
Third, purely volumetric counts:shots, generic possession share, total tackles,show in-consistent or context-dependent value once quality or context is considered.
Despite this progress, three methodological weaknesses remain: 1. Static threshold assumptions. Existing studies report competition-level cut-offs (e.g., a +0.8 xG advantage or PPDA ≤10) but do not allow those break-17 points to vary by team or to be estimated jointly across several KPIs.
2. Untested synergies.
While possession share, direct play, progressive entry and finishing efficiency each matter individually, their combined, or potentially offsetting, effects on win probability have not been modelled.
3. Neglect of distributional tails.
Almost all work focuses on mean effects; little is known about how KPIs behave in the upper quantiles that correspond to high-confidence wins or heavy defeats.
Study aims To address these gaps, the dissertation pursues three objectives: 1. Threshold estimation: Develop a Bayesian hierarchical framework that esti-mates team-specific change-points for key attacking, progression and defensive KPIs.
2. Interaction testing: Include two exploratory cross-terms-PossessionDiff × DirectnessDiff and AttackingActionsDiff × FinishingEffDiff to examine whether combinations of territorial dominance and conversion skill yield non-additive effects.
3. Distributional coverage: Employ hierarchical quantile regression to model the full conditional distribution of win probability, with particular attention to the 90th percentiles that coaches and analysts use for scenario planning.
Chapter 3 details the modelling strategy; Chapters 4 and 5 present the empirical results and their practical implications.
3 Data and Methods 3.1 Data and Feature Preparation 3.1.1 Data Source and Collection This study is based on publicly available match-level data collected from FBref.com, which provides advanced football metrics via StatsBomb. The dataset comprises all 18 matches from the English Premier League between the 2020/2021 and 2023/2024 sea-sons, yielding approximately 1,500 observations. Each observation corresponds to a single match and contains detailed performance indicators for both home and away teams.
A summary of the collected variables: Variable Name Description Type Home Team Name of the home team Categorical Away Team Name of the away team Categorical Home Goals Goals scored by the home team Numerical Away Goals Goals scored by the away team Numerical xG (Home) Expected goals for the home team Numerical xG (Away) Expected goals for the away team Numerical Possession (Home) Possession percentage of the home team Numerical Possession (Away) Possession percentage of the away team Numerical Defensive Actions Total defensive actions per team Numerical Attacking Actions Total attacking actions per team Numerical Cards Number of yellow and red cards per team Numerical Finishing Efficiency Shot-to-goal conversion rate per team Numerical Directness Verticality of team play (constructed metric) Numerical Table 1: Summary of collected variables 3.1.2 Target Variable Definition The primary target variable in this analysis is the probability that the home team will win the match. Rather than modelling this as a binary classification task, the objective is to estimate the 90th percentile of the home win probability distribution, thereby encompassing matches in which conditions are particularly conducive to the home team’s success. This tail-focused modelling approach facilitates the acquisition of tactical insights into high-confidence scenarios.
19 3.1.3 Predictor Selection and Feature Engineering In order to reflect tactical dynamics, a set of key performance indicators (KPIs) were selected and transformed into differential metrics between the home and away teams.
The following predictors have been selected for further analysis: • xGDiff: Difference in expected goals • PossessionDiff: Difference in possession percentages • DefActionsDiff: Difference in defensive actions • AttackingActionsDiff: Difference in attacking actions • CardIntensityDiff: Difference in normalized card counts • DirectnessDiff: Constructed metric reflecting the verticality of play • FinishingEffDiff: Constructed metric reflecting shot-to-goal conversion effi-ciency To capture nonlinear interactions, two additional features were engineered: i1 = PossessionDiff ×DirectnessDiff i2 = AttackingActionsDiff × FinishingEffDiff The final feature matrix thus includes 9 predictor variables.
In order to account for the tactical synergies between match statistics, two in-teraction terms were introduced: one between PossessionDiff and DirectnessDiff, and another between AttackingActionsDiff and FinishingEffDiff. These were chosen based on domain intuition about how certain styles of play combine to shape high win proba-bility events. Importantly, the inclusion of interaction terms in regression-type models is supported by the literature as a parsimonious way to model complex, nonlinear relationships without overparameterization. Fong (2015) emphasise that interaction terms are particularly valuable when threshold or change-point behaviour is expected to emerge only under specific configurations of predictors, which may be masked if only main effects are considered. In the context of Bayesian quantile regression, the strate-gic limitation of interactions is instrumental in the management of dimensionality and 20 the prevention of suboptimal convergence behaviour (e.g., collinearity or inflated vari-ance). This principle assumes particular significance in the utilisation of MCMC-based inference.
3.1.4 Team Index Encoding and Grouping To incorporate team-specific variability in the modeling process, each home team was assigned a unique integer index. This encoding allows for the estimation of team-level random effects via hierarchical priors. The indices are stored in a vector team idx, which maps each match to its corresponding home team group. This structure supports group-level modeling for both intercept shifts and change-point thresholds later in the model specification.
3.2 Quantile Regression and Change-Point Modelling The present study employs Bayesian Quantile Regression (BQR) to model football outcomes at the 90th percentile of win probability, a choice motivated by the need to understand performance under peak conditions. Unlike traditional mean regression, quantile regression enables the analyst to investigate the relationship between predic-tors and the conditional distribution of the response variable at any specified quantile level. This approach is of particular value in the field of football analytics, where in-stances of extreme performance , such as dominant wins ,frequently possess a greater strategic significance than more average trends.
The statistical foundation of BQR rests on the work of Yu and Moyeed (2001), who introduced a likelihood-based Bayesian formulation using the Asymmetric Laplace Dis-tribution (ALD). The ALD conveniently links the τ-th quantile of the response to a likelihood, allowing inference via standard Bayesian tools.
Kozumi and Kobayashi (2011) enhanced the computational efficiency by formulating a Gibbs sampling scheme for BQR with the ALD. Building on this, Hu et al. (2013) developed a hierarchical Bayesian quantile regression model, showing how posterior inference using MCMC can be extended to more flexible functional forms.
These contributions collectively en-able the extraction of full posterior distributions at arbitrary quantiles, offering cred-ible intervals and uncertainty quantification for model parameters, a crucial asset in performance-sensitive domains like sports analytics.
21 In order to enhance the model’s relevance in capturing tactical shifts, a team-specific change-point mechanism is introduced on the xG differential (xGDiff) predictor. This enables the model to detect latent thresholds at which the marginal effect of xGDiff on win probability shifts. For instance, it can identify inflection points where a slight increase in attacking dominance leads to a disproportionately higher probability of victory for a given team.
The methodological approach is theoretically founded on the work of Jia et al.
(2021), who proposed a Bayesian Change-Point Quantile Regression (BCPQR) model that integrates change-point detection with quantile estimation. The proposed frame-work facilitates the identification of structural breaks in regression parameters, thereby enabling the variation of intercepts or slopes once the predictor crosses a latent thresh-old. The model is tailored to reveal heterogeneous responses across different quantile levels, providing nuanced insight into when and how predictive relationships shift.
Psioda et al. (2022) extend this framework further by incorporating random effects and group-specific variation, demonstrating how change-points can differ across obser-vational units, a feature particularly useful in modelling team-specific dynamics.
Overall, the BQR model with embedded change-points enables tailored quantile estimation for each team, highlighting asymmetric tactical responses.
3.3 Model Specification This section formally defines the two probabilistic models used in the analysis: one without change-points (No-CP) and one with team-specific change-points (CP) on the xGDiff predictor.
Both models are constructed within a Bayesian hierarchical framework, enabling partial pooling across teams and probabilistic inference over all parameters.
The models are built using the Asymmetric Laplace Distribution (ALD) as the like-lihood function, which aligns with the pinball loss used in classical quantile regression.
This formulation is standard in the field of Bayesian quantile regression literature due to its ability to directly model a target quantile whilst producing full posterior distri-butions over all parameters.
22 3.3.1 No-Change-Point (No-CP) Model The No-CP model estimates the conditional 90th percentile of home win probability using a linear formulation: yi ∼ALD �µi, σ, τ = 0.9  , µi = α + xT i β + ut(i) (1) Where: • yi is the 90th percentile of home win probability for match i • xi ∈RP is the vector of predictors • α is the global intercept • β are regression coefficients • ut(i) ∼N(0, σ2 team) is the team-specific random intercept • σ is the scale parameter of the ALD This model accounts for latent team-specific effects using a normally distributed random intercept per team. The hierarchical structure enables the model to borrow strength across teams, which is particularly useful for teams with fewer matches.
3.3.2 Change-Point (CP) Model To capture nonlinear shifts in match dynamics, the CP model introduces a team-specific threshold on the xGDiff predictor. The idea is that performance impact might change sharply when a team’s expected goals exceed a latent tactical turning point.
yi ∼ALD �µi, σ, τ = 0.9  , µi = α + xT i β + ut(i) + γ S �xi,xGDiff, θt(i)  , (2) 23 Where: S(x, θ) = sigmoid �λ (x −θ)  (3) • θt(i) ∼N(µθ, σ2 θ) is the team-specific threshold on xGDiff • γ is the global effect size of crossing the threshold • λ is a steepness parameter (set empirically, e.g., 20) The CP model flexibly adjusts the expected impact of xGDiff based on whether a team’s performance exceeds its latent threshold θt(i). This accommodates the intuition that some teams respond more aggressively to high attacking output than others. The sigmoid function ensures the transition is smooth and differentiable, which facilitates inference via Hamiltonian methods.
Both models are implemented in Pyro and sampled using the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS). A custom implementation of the ALD likelihood is used to maintain coherence with quantile-based inference goals.
3.4 Implementation and Diagnostics 3.4.1 Inference via MCMC and NUTS Sampler The estimation of models is performed using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), im-plemented through the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS), a variant of Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) that adaptively tunes step size and path length. Hoffman and Gelman (2014) showed this method is particularly effective for high-dimensional Bayesian mod-els, as it improves convergence while maintaining theoretical guarantees of posterior consistency.
The models have been implemented in Pyro, a probabilistic programming language built on PyTorch. Each model is run with: • Two chains 24 • 500 warmup steps • 500 posterior samples per chain The Pyro backend ensures automatic differentiation, flexible model specification, and reproducible sampling.
3.4.2 Posterior Summary and Model Fit Checks The posterior samples obtained from MCMC are summarized using the ArviZ library (Kumar et al., 2019). For each model, we compute: • Posterior means and medians • Highest Density Intervals (HDIs) • Marginal distributions for key parameters Posterior predictive checks are performed to assess how well the model reproduces observed data characteristics. These include visual comparisons of predicted vs. actual values and analysis of residuals to identify systematic misfit.
3.4.3 Convergence Diagnostics Convergence is evaluated using a combination of quantitative and visual diagnostics: • ˆ R (R-hat) statistics: Values below 1.01 are taken as evidence of convergence.
• Effective Sample Size (ESS): Used to assess the quality of posterior sampling.
• Trace plots: Visual examination of MCMC chains to detect non-convergent be-havior or pathological sampling.
These diagnostics ensure that posterior inferences are valid and not artifacts of poor sampler behavior. The tools used follow best practices in Bayesian modeling workflows and are implemented via ArviZ.
25 4 Results 4.1 Model Comparison and Predictive Performance This section presents the core evaluation of model performance using quantile loss at the 90th percentile (τ = 0.90), the central metric selected to reflect the model’s ability to capture upper-bound win probabilities. This is a crucial perspective in performance modelling, where capturing high-probability outliers is often more informative than modelling the mean.
To evaluate model performance, two models were compared: • No-Change-Point (No-CP) Bayesian quantile regression with hierarchical team-specific intercepts.
• Change-Point (CP) extension, adding team-specific thresholds on the primary predictor (xGDiff), with a shared CP effect βcp.
The quantile loss is defined as: L(τ) = X i ρτ �yi −ˆ yi  , ρτ(u) = u �τ −I{u < 0}  (4) It was computed over the full dataset.
Lower values indicate better predictive performance for the chosen quantile.
26 Figure 1: Quantile Loss Comparison Between Models As observed in Figure 1, the No-CP model yields a slightly lower quantile loss (182.52) in comparison to the CP model (182.79). This marginal difference indicates that the added complexity of modelling team-specific change-points does not improve predictive performance at the 90th percentile. Indeed, the CP model exhibits sub-optimal performance, despite the requirement for a substantially greater number of parameters and computational time.
The result of this study carries with it several implications.
• Parsimony over complexity: The simpler No-CP model generalizes better on the full dataset, emphasizing that not all flexible structures, such as change-points, necessarily yield better real-world predictions.
• Model regularization: The Bayesian framework penalizes overfitting through priors; the poor gain in loss reduction from CP may reflect that the signal cap-tured by team-specific thresholds was weak or highly uncertain.
• Interpretation of non-significance: While the CP model is theoretically richer, its failure to outperform under this evaluation suggests that any nonlinear 27 xGDiff thresholds are either poorly estimated or not systematically present in the data.
Computational Considerations While the CP model was designed to capture non-linear shifts in xGDiff, its computational demands were significantly higher than the No-CP model. On identical hardware, the No-CP model completed inference in under 1 hour, whereas the CP model required over 7 hours due to its higher-dimensional latent structure and weaker convergence behaviour. Additionally, the CP model ex-hibited worse convergence diagnostics, with multiple parameters showing elevated ˆ R values (greater than 1.10) and low effective sample sizes, despite increased warmup and sampling iterations. These factors suggest that the added model complexity incurs sub-stantial computational and inferential costs without meaningful predictive gain.
4.2 Change-Point Threshold Analysis While the overall performance of the CP model did not surpass that of its simpler counterpart, an analysis of its internal parameters provides valuable insight into the behaviour of threshold-based mechanisms at a team level.
Global Threshold Dynamics The model includes a global threshold parameter (µthr) representing the average change-point in the xGDiff variable. It is expected that match dominance (in terms of expected goals difference) will significantly increase win probability beyond this point. The posterior median estimate for µthr is 0.27, with a high standard deviation (±0.26), indicating substantial uncertainty in the location of this global threshold.
This lack of precision is reinforced by the σthr estimate of 1.28, suggesting consider-able dispersion in the team-specific thresholds. Rather than clustering around a shared turning point, teams appear to diverge meaningfully in how they respond to xGDiff changes.
4.3 Summary of Findings and Implications Key Findings 28 • No-CP Outperformed CP in Predictive Accuracy: Despite the theoret-ical appeal of including latent thresholds, the No-CP model exhibited slightly lower quantile loss and significantly better convergence diagnostics. This finding suggests that the simpler model is more generalizable and efficient in practice.
• Change-Points Were Not Meaningfully Identified: The CP model esti-mated wide posterior intervals for the global threshold (µthr = 0.27 ± 0.26) and team-specific thresholds (σthr = 1.28), indicating weak signal strength and high uncertainty. The estimated effect of passing the threshold (βc = −0.07 ± 0.98) was also negligible, further reducing confidence in the model’s added value.
• The team-level interpretations remain inconclusive: The CP model posits the hypothesis that certain teams may function with varying dominance thresh-olds (γteam). However, the instability of these estimates across teams and chains hinders their interpretability. It was not possible to draw compelling tactical insights from these parameters.
• Feature Effects Were Consistent with Prior Football Knowledge: Pre-dictors such as xGDiff, PossessionDiff and FinishingEffDiff demonstrated con-sistent positive contributions to high win probability, thereby confirming their importance. However, variables such as AttackingActions and DefensiveActions, while intuitive, exhibited wider intervals, reflecting team-level variability in play styles and success conversion.
• The computational cost was found to be substantial: The CP model re-quired over 7 hours of inference and still exhibited suboptimal convergence, while the No-CP model completed in under 1 hour with clean diagnostics. This prompts pragmatic considerations concerning the scalability of change-point modelling in sports datasets of this magnitude and granularity.
Implications From a modelling standpoint, these findings support the principle of parsimony, i.e. the use of models that are simpler but stable, interpretable, and well-validated often outperform more complex alternatives that introduce uncertain latent structures. In the field of applied football analytics, this insight holds particular rel-evance for practitioners seeking to construct robust predictive instruments without compromising interpretability or computational feasibility.
29 From a tactical perspective, the absence of reliable threshold effects calls into ques-tion the assumption that teams exhibit clear behavioural shifts at specific performance tipping points (e.g. xGDiff ¿ X). Instead, the path to high win probability appears to be more gradual and influenced by a combination of statistical dominance, conversion efficiency, and potentially unmodeled contextual factors (e.g., form, substitutions, or opponent quality).
Overall, this chapter demonstrates the power—and limits—of Bayesian hierarchical modeling in capturing the nuances of match success. The insights derived from this study lay the foundation for future refinements, which may be achieved through the utilisation of alternative priors, the incorporation of richer temporal modelling, or the integration of in-game context.
Figure 2: Team-Specific (γteam) Thresholds Team-Specific Thresholds As illustrated in Figure 2, the distribution of team-specific change-point estimates (γteam) have been sorted according to magnitude. These 30 values represent the minimum xGDiff required to trigger a step-change in win prob-ability for each team.
Some clubs (e.g., Liverpool, Manchester City) exhibit lower thresholds, implying that even modest statistical superiority may suffice to signal a decisive game advantage. In contrast, teams such as Burnley or Bournemouth require higher xGDiff margins before the model identifies a turning point, which may be in-dicative of a more volatile or inefficient conversion of dominance into results. However, the posterior uncertainty surrounding these γteam values remains substantial in many cases. This challenges their interpretability and reliability from a tactical standpoint.
In other words, while the model attempts to learn where thresholds lie, it struggles to pin them down with confidence, particularly for mid- or lower-table teams with more erratic match dynamics.
Change-Point Effect Size The parameter βcp, which quantifies the jump in win probability once the xGDiff surpasses the threshold, has a median estimate of −0.07, with a wide 90% HDI spanning both negative and positive regions (±0.98). This once more reflects an insignificant effect, thereby suggesting that, even if thresholds exist, the magnitude of change in the outcome is negligible and highly uncertain.
Taken together, these results imply that team-specific change-points on xGDiff are not robustly learned in this dataset and modeling setup. This might be due to factors such as high intra-team variability across matches and seasons, or the limited sensitivity of win probability to a single tactical KPI like xGDiff.
Nevertheless, the attempt to include latent change-point structures highlights an important modeling direction. Even if not impactful in this instance, threshold model-ing remains a promising concept for tactical segmentation in sports analytics, provided future models integrate richer data or regularization schemes.
4.4 Marginal Effects of xGDiff and Model Interpretability A core objective of this study was to model how differences in expected goals (xGDiff) affect a team’s likelihood of winning under high-performance conditions. While the No-CP model ultimately proved more effective, both models yield useful insights into the functional relationship between xGDiff and upper-bound win probability.
The 31 relationship between these variables is illustrated in Figure 3, which plots the predicted win probabilities against xGDiff under both the No-CP and CP models.
Figure 3: Effect of xGDiff With and Without Change-Points The No-CP model (solid green line) yields a smooth, nearly linear increase in pre-dicted 90th-percentile win probability as xGDiff increases.
This result supports a simple interpretation: teams that outperform opponents in expected goals consistently increase their odds of winning—no sudden shifts or nonlinear jumps are necessary to explain this dynamic.
In contrast, the CP model (illustrated by the dashed red line) introduces a step-like discontinuity at the average learned threshold, beyond which the effect of xGDiff intensifies. While this nonlinear jump is visually compelling, it appears to exaggerate the impact of marginal increases in xGDiff beyond the estimated cut-point. Given that this added flexibility did not yield better predictive performance (Section 4.1), it is likely modeling noise rather than a genuine tactical phenomenon.
From a football analytics perspective, these findings underscore the importance of employing xGDiff as a continuous, interpretable metric for performance evaluation.
The absence of a strong or consistent threshold supports the view that match domi-nance is better represented as a gradient, where accumulating xG advantage steadily improves win prospects, rather than triggering sudden changes at arbitrary breakpoints.
32 4.5 Posterior Coefficients and Feature Relevance Beyond the evaluation of overall predictive performance and threshold behaviour, it is equally important to understand the role of each input variable in shaping predicted win probabilities. This section is concerned with a comparison of the posterior distributions of model coefficients for both the No-CP and CP models. The focus is on the direction, magnitude, and uncertainty of each feature’s effect.
Figure 4: Posterior Distributions of Feature Coefficients (CP vs. No-CP) The figure presents the posterior means and 90% Highest Density Intervals (HDIs) for each feature under both models. The blue dots represent the CP model, whilst the orange dots represent the No-CP model.
Key Findings and Interpretation It is evident that xGDiff is the strongest predictor in both models, with a consistently large and positive effect on the 90th-percentile win probability. This finding, paired with the strong positive effects for both DirectnessDiff and FinishingEffDiff, serves to reinforce the assertion that offensive dominance as well as vertical attacking playstyles 33 and higher conversion rates, exert a pivotal role in determining the outcome of sporting matches.
The positive values of both CardIntensityDiff and AttackingActionsDiff result in wider intervals under the CP model.
This finding suggests that greater parameter uncertainty is present due to the added model complexity.
The seasonal indicators for the years 2022–23 and 2023–24 have been analysed, with the results indicating consistent downward shifts, potentially reflecting more competi-tive parity or variance in tactical effectiveness across seasons.
PossessionDiff and DefActionsDiff have posterior distributions centered near zero in both models. This points to limited predictive power when controlling for more direct indicators like xGDiff, reaffirming previous literature that possession alone does not guarantee dominance or success at extreme quantile levels.
Football Relevance From a practical standpoint, these coefficient estimates highlight which metrics coaches and analysts might prioritize when aiming to push team performance into the top 10% of winning scenarios. Variables such as xGDiff, Directness, and Finishing Efficiency stand out as consistently impactful, providing support for tactical models focused on shot quality and verticality rather than merely maintaining possession.
Modeling Implication This analysis reinforces that the No-CP model not only performs slightly better overall, but it also delivers tighter and more stable coefficient estimates. The CP model in-troduces volatility and wider High-Dimensional Integrals (HDIs) in many variables—a likely consequence of its added complexity and weaker convergence.
34 4.6 Summary of Findings and Implications 4.6.1 Key Findings No-CP Outperformed CP in Predictive Accuracy. Despite the theoretical ap-peal of including latent thresholds, the No-CP model exhibited slightly lower quantile loss and significantly better convergence diagnostics. This finding suggests that the simpler model is more generalizable and efficient in practice.
Change-Points Were Not Meaningfully Identified. The CP model estimated wide posterior intervals for the global threshold (µthr = 0.27 ± 0.26) and team-specific thresholds (σthr = 1.28), indicating weak signal strength and high uncertainty. The estimated effect of passing the threshold (βc = −0.07±0.98) was also negligible, further reducing confidence in the model’s added value.
The team-level interpretations remain inconclusive. The CP model posits the hypothesis that certain teams may function with varying dominance thresholds (γteam). However, the instability of these estimates across teams and chains hinders their interpretability. It was not possible to draw compelling tactical insights from these parameters.
Feature Effects Were Consistent with Prior Football Knowledge. Pre-dictors such as xGDiff, PossessionDiff and FinishingEffDiff demonstrated consistent positive contributions to high win probability, thereby confirming their importance.
However, variables such as AttackingActions and DefensiveActions, while intuitive, exhibited wider intervals, reflecting team-level variability in play styles and success conversion.
The computational cost was found to be substantial. The CP model required over 7 hours of inference and still exhibited suboptimal convergence, while the No-CP model completed in under 1 hour with clean diagnostics.
This prompts pragmatic considerations concerning the scalability of change-point modelling in sports datasets of this magnitude and granularity.
35 4.6.2 Implications From a modelling standpoint, these findings support the principle of parsimony, i.e., the use of models that are simpler but stable, interpretable, and well-validated of-ten outperform more complex alternatives that introduce uncertain latent structures.
In the field of applied football analytics, this insight holds particular relevance for practitioners seeking to construct robust predictive instruments without compromising interpretability or computational feasibility.
From a tactical perspective, the absence of reliable threshold effects calls into ques-tion the assumption that teams exhibit clear behavioural shifts at specific performance tipping points (e.g., xGDiff > X). Instead, the path to high win probability appears to be more gradual and influenced by a combination of statistical dominance, conversion efficiency, and potentially unmodeled contextual factors (e.g., form, substitutions, or opponent quality).
Overall, this chapter demonstrates the power—and limits—of Bayesian hierarchical modeling in capturing the nuances of match success. The insights derived from this study lay the foundation for future refinements, which may be achieved through the utilisation of alternative priors, the incorporation of richer temporal modelling, or the integration of in-game context.
5 Conclusion This study set out to model the 90th-percentile win probability in the English Pre-mier League using Bayesian quantile regression, with an emphasis on capturing high-performance conditions via match-level key performance indicators (KPIs). Two hier-archical models were constructed: a parsimonious baseline (No-CP) and a structurally richer extension incorporating team-specific change-points (CP).
5.1 Summary of Findings This study set out to predict the 90th-percentile win probability in the English Premier League with Bayesian quantile regression and to test whether adding team-specific 36 change-points on xG difference (xGDiff) would sharpen forecasts and tactical insights.
The findings can be summarised as follows: • Model Performance: : The simpler No-Change-Point (No-CP) specification achieved the lowest quantile-loss score and ran markedly faster than the Change-Point (CP) alternative, indicating that a parsimonious structure is adequate for estimating high-end win probabilities.
• Change-Point Inference: Team-level thresholds (γteam) proved highly uncer-tain and heterogeneous, while the global threshold displayed a wide posterior and the associated jump effect (βcp) was practically zero. Premier-League sides therefore show no evidence of uniform tactical tipping points in xG dominance.
• Team Effects: Hierarchical intercepts captured persistent differences in base-line strength more reliably than the change-points, confirming their value for modelling club-level variation.
• Feature importance: xGDiff and DirectnessDiff were the dominant covariates.
Cross-terms such as Possession × Directness offered minor refinements but did not alter the predictor hierarchy, and volume-based measures like AttackingAc-tionsDiff contributed little at the 90th-percentile level.
• Diagnostics: The No-CP model exhibited superior convergence and effective sample sizes, whereas the CP specification required longer chains without yielding practical gains.
The findings, when considered as a whole, indicate that while more complex mod-els with latent thresholds may offer a stronger conceptual foundation, simpler, well-regularized models demonstrate superior generalizability in practical applications, par-ticularly when confronted with constraints such as limited sample sizes, noise, and model identifiability issues. From both a statistical and applied football analytics per-spective, the threshold effects in xGDiff appear to be too unstable to justify their complexity under the current data and assumptions.
37 5.2 Theoretical and Practical Implications This research contributes to both methodological innovation in sports analytics and practical insights for football decision-makers. From a theoretical standpoint, the study demonstrates that Bayesian quantile regression can be a viable framework for estimat-ing upper-bound win probabilities, capturing aspects of performance beyond mean-based modelling. However, the study also demonstrates the limitations of increased complexity. The incorporation of team-specific change points did not enhance pre-dictive accuracy, indicating that overfitting and weak signal strength are significant concerns when modelling tactical thresholds.
Practically, these findings challenge the idea that every club must calibrate its own bespoke xG-based threshold. The simpler No-CP model not only delivered the best quantile-loss score but also converged more quickly, indicating that a league-wide specification built on transparent KPIs such as expected goals is both reliable and interpretable for match analysis. Because the model updates cleanly with streaming event data, analysts can embed it in a live tactical dashboard: win-probability quantiles and KPI alerts refresh every few seconds, flagging when their side drops below target.
The same engine can also run in pre-match mode,using historical priors and projected line-ups, to generate scenario forecasts that inform game planning, squad rotation and broadcast graphics.
The findings of this study lend support to the utilisation of parsimonious, trans-parent models in tactical evaluation, thereby reinforcing the value of quantile-based performance evaluation, particularly in the context of understanding high-performing outcomes. For coaches, the results suggest that excessive tailoring of thresholds may be unwarranted unless supported by strong evidence, and that broad trends across teams might be more useful for strategic planning than assumed.
5.3 Limitations and future work 5.3.1 Limitations While the methodological framework applied in this thesis offers a structured and probabilistically grounded approach to modeling high win probability scenarios, several limitations must be acknowledged to provide a complete understanding of the results.
38 Firstly, the incorporation of team-specific change-points resulted in a considerable increase in computational expense without yielding a quantifiable enhancement in pre-dictive efficacy. The CP model required over seven hours of sampling and exhibited inferior convergence diagnostics relative to the No-CP model. This raises questions about the practical feasibility of such models when applied to datasets of similar scale.
Secondly, the posterior distributions for team-specific threshold parameters were wide, and the estimated global threshold effect was not statistically distinguishable from zero. This suggests that either the data lacked the resolution necessary to support threshold identification or that such inflection points are not present in the underlying match dynamics, at least for the xGDiff variable.
Thirdly, the modelling framework, while hierarchical, remains cross-sectional. That is to say, it does not explicitly incorporate temporal dynamics, such as changes in squad composition, form fluctuations, or mid-season tactical adjustments. These factors may play a significant role in match outcomes and are likely to interact with performance indicators in a way that static models cannot fully capture.
Fourthly, the scope of performance indicators was limited to match-level KPIs extracted from public data. While this ensures reproducibility, it restricts the abil-ity to analyse micro-level dynamics such as pressing patterns, positional tracking, or opponent-specific adjustments, which may be critical in high-performance scenarios.
Finally, the model did not incorporate explicit measures of uncertainty in data an-notation or external contextual variables, such as referee decisions, weather conditions, or match importance, all of which have the potential to influence win probability.
5.3.2 Future Directions While the present study contributes a quantile-focused Bayesian modelling framework for estimating football match success, several extensions remain to be explored in future research.
One promising direction involves the incorporation of temporal dynamics into the hierarchical structure.
The current model treats all observations as exchangeable, thereby ignoring within-season trends, roster changes, injuries, or evolving tactical strategies. The integration of time-aware components, such as autoregressive effects, 39 season-specific intercepts, or time-varying coefficients, may facilitate the capture of performance evolution across the football calendar, thereby enhancing sensitivity to short-term form.
Another avenue that merits exploration concerns the estimation of change points under stronger priors or regularisation schemes. The present implementation relied on weakly informative priors; however, alternative formulations using sparsity-inducing or hierarchical shrinkage priors could mitigate the uncertainty observed in this study and stabilise inference for team-specific thresholds, if such effects exist. This would necessitate meticulous calibration and substantiation, although it could potentially generate more robust insights in contexts where breakpoints are theorised a priori.
The incorporation of richer contextual variables signifies an opportunity for en-hancement. The match-level predictors employed in this study were constrained to general performance indicators, such as expected goals (xG), possession, and defensive actions. The incorporation of additional variables, such as opponent strength, player availability, match importance, and in-game events, is a potential avenue for future models. These variables may help explain residual variance and better account for context-sensitive match dynamics.
Additionally, future research may explore cross-league or multi-competition appli-cations to assess the generalizability of the modelling approach. Applying the same framework to other domestic leagues or international tournaments could help identify whether the absence of threshold effects observed in this study is specific to the Premier League or a broader property of high-level football.
Finally, as modeling frameworks grow in complexity, attention should be given to communicability and applied relevance.
Developing methods to translate posterior summaries into accessible benchmarks, such as team reports or scenario-based dash-boards, could support the practical integration of quantile-based analytics into coaching and recruitment workflows.
